## Short Answer Questions

1. $\frac{1}{{ }_{g} n_{a}}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}$
${ }_{a} n_{g}=\frac{1}{{ }_{g} n_{a}}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-064.jpg?height=298&width=380&top_left_y=886&top_left_x=591)

But $\angle \mathrm{y}=\angle \mathrm{r}[$ alt. $\angle \mathrm{s}]$
$\angle x=\angle i[\angle$ of emergence $=\angle$ of incidence $]$
i.e. ${ }_{g} n_{a}=\frac{\sin r}{\sin i}$
Taking reciprocal, $\frac{1}{{ }_{g} n_{a}}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}$

From (1) \& (2), ${ }_{a} n_{g}=\frac{1}{{ }_{g} n_{a}}$
2. When white light passes through a transparent medium like a glass prism it splits into seven colours. This phenomenon is called as dispersion of light.
The dispersion of light occurs due to different speeds of different colours when it passes through a glass prism.
3. The power of this lens has negative (-) sign, so it is a concave lens.

Power, $P=\frac{1}{f(\text { in } \mathrm{m})}$
$\therefore-2.0=\frac{1}{f} \quad$ or $\quad f=\frac{1}{-2.0} \mathrm{~m} \quad$ or $f=-0.50 \mathrm{~m}$
∴ Focal length, $f=-0.50 \mathrm{~m}$
4. The image will be virtual and erect if $u<F$, the image will be smaller if $u>2 F$ and larger if $u<2 F$.
5. Let the distance between boy and miror by x .

Boy $x$ Mirror $x$ Image
i.e. $x+x=20 \Rightarrow 2 x=20 \Rightarrow x=10 \mathrm{~m}$
6. Refractive index in inversely proportional to the wavelength. The least wavelength (violet) is deflected the most so the decreasing order of refractive index will be $\mu_{r}<\mu_{y}<\mu_{g}$.
7. Given : Object distance $u=-12 \mathrm{~cm}$.

Magnification $m=-4$.
We know that, $m=-\frac{v}{u}=\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}$
$\therefore \quad-4=-\frac{v}{u}=-\frac{v}{(-12)} \quad \therefore \quad v=-48 \mathrm{~cm}$
So, image is formed at a distance of 48 cm on the same side of object.
8. Given : ${ }_{\text {air }} n_{\text {ice }}=1.31,{ }_{\text {air }} n_{\text {rock }}=1.54$
${ }_{\text {ice }} n_{\text {rock }}=\frac{\text { air } n_{\text {rock }}}{{ }_{\text {air }} n_{\text {ice }}}=\frac{1.54}{1.31}=1.18$.
9. There will be no change in the focal length of the mirrors when immersed in water because the focal length of the mirrors does not depend on the external in which it is held. In case of convex lens, focal length increases when immersed in water, the focal length of a line depends on the surrounding fluid because lens working on refraction of light.
10. The lens is placed along the path of ligth-either sun rays or through electric lamp.
The rays converge at a point. Find the distance between converging point and lens which gives the rough focal length of the convex lens.
11. Convex mirror
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-064.jpg?height=378&width=584&top_left_y=1388&top_left_x=1146)

Use of convex mirror-as rear view mirror in vehicles because its field of view is larger and it forms virtual erect and diminished images of objects behind.
12. (i) Ability of lens to converge or diverge the light rays.
(ii) + ve sign → converging lens/convex lens - ve sign → diverging lens/concave lens
(iii) S.I. unit of power is dioptre 1 dioptre $=1 /$ focal length $(\mathrm{m})$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-064.jpg?height=442&width=776&top_left_y=2101&top_left_x=1050)
13. We know that mirror equation,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-15}=\frac{1}{-10} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{-10}+\frac{1}{15} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{-10}+\frac{1}{15} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{v}=\frac{(-15+10)}{150}=\frac{-5}{150} \\
& v=-30 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus a sharp image of the object will obtain If we place the screen in front of the mirror at a distance of 30 cm from the mirror.
Magnification is given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
& m=\frac{-v}{u}=\frac{h_{1}}{h_{0}} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & \frac{-30}{-15}=\frac{h_{1}}{4} \\
& -2=\frac{h}{4} \\
\Rightarrow \quad & h_{1}=-8 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

The height of image will be 8 cm .
14. Four properties of image formed by the given convex mirror are:
(i) Image is always erect.
(ii) Smaller in size
(iii) Virtual
(iv) Always form behind the mirror between focus and pole
15. Convex mirror. It forms an image smaller than that of the object.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-065.jpg?height=378&width=729&top_left_y=1932&top_left_x=274)
Long Answer Questions

1. Radius of curvature of convex mirror, $R=30 \mathrm{~cm}$.
∴ Focal length of convex mirror, $f=\frac{R}{2}=\frac{30 \mathrm{~cm}}{2}=15 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Now $h=5 \mathrm{~cm}, u=-20 \mathrm{~cm}, v=?, h^{\prime}=$ ?

Using the mirror formula $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}$, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{-20}=\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{20}=\frac{4+3}{60} \\
& v=\frac{60}{7}=8.6 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, image is formed at a distance of 8.6 cm behind the convex mirror. The image is virtual and erect.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& m=\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}=-\frac{v}{u} \\
& \frac{h^{\prime}}{5}=-\frac{8.6}{-20} \Rightarrow h^{\prime}=\frac{8.6}{20} \times 5=2.15 \mathrm{~cm} .
\end{aligned}
$$

So the image size is diminished.
2. For convex mirror, we have given
$u=-10 \mathrm{~m}, R=2.0 \mathrm{~m}$
So, $f=\frac{R}{2}=\frac{2.0 \mathrm{~m}}{2}=1.0 \mathrm{~m}$
Using the mirror formula, $\frac{1}{u}+\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}$
We get $\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{1.0}+\frac{1}{10}=\frac{11}{10}$
or $v=\frac{10}{11}=0.9 \mathrm{~m}$
Thus, the car would appear at 0.9 m from the convex mirror. We know that $m=-\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-\frac{10}{11}}{-10}=\frac{1}{11}$
Thus, size of the image of the car will be $\frac{1}{11}$ times smaller the actual size of the car through the convex mirror.
3. According to cartesian sign convention,
$u=-90 \mathrm{~cm}, f=-30 \mathrm{~cm}$.
We have the formula $\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{90}=-\frac{1}{30}$
or $\frac{1}{v}=-\frac{1}{30}+\frac{1}{90}=\frac{-3+1}{90}=\frac{-2}{90}$
$\therefore \quad v=-45 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Magnification,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& m=\frac{-v}{u}=\frac{-(-45)}{-90} \\
& m=-\frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

A real, smaller than object and inverted image is formed 45 cm from the mirror on the same side as the object.
4. 60 cm .
5. $-10 \mathrm{~cm},-10 \mathrm{D}$
6. Mirror formula is, $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}$ where $u, v$ and $f$ are object distance, image distance and focal length.

$$
\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u}
$$

Focal length and object distance are both $-v e$ for concave mirror.
It is given $u<f$,
$\therefore \frac{1}{v}$ becomes $\frac{-1}{f}+\frac{1}{u}$ and is positive.
$v$ becoming + ve denotes a virtual image.
7. An extended object $A B$ is placed in front of a plane mirror $M M^{\prime}$. A light ray from $A$ falls perpendicular on a mirror at $P$. So it comes back along the same path after reflection. Another ray $A Q(Q$ is a point of normal from $B)$, falls on the mirror and after reflection it goes along the path $Q R$. When these two reflected rays i.e. $P A \& Q R$ are produced backward, they meet at a point $A^{\prime}$. Similarly, the point $B^{\prime}$ is also obtained. It means $A^{\prime} B^{\prime}$ is the image of AB . This image is virtual and erect which forms as far behind the mirror as the object is in front of it. i.e. $A P=A^{\prime} P$.
$B Q=B^{\prime} Q$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-066.jpg?height=337&width=517&top_left_y=1340&top_left_x=310)

To prove : $A B=A^{\prime} B^{\prime}$.
$\triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} Q=\angle A^{\prime} Q B^{\prime},=\angle r$
(Vertically opposite angles)
$\angle i=\angle r$ (laws of reflection)
∴ In $\triangle A B Q$ and $\triangle A^{\prime} Q B^{\prime}, \angle i=\angle A^{\prime} Q B^{\prime}$
$\angle A B Q=\angle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} Q=90^{\circ}$
$B Q=B^{\prime} Q \quad$ (because distance of image behind the mirror = distance of object in front of the mirror)
$\therefore \triangle A B Q \cong \triangle A^{\prime} B^{\prime} Q$. (ASA).
$\therefore A B=A^{\prime} B^{\prime}(C P C T)$ i.e. size of object $=$ size of image.
8. (a) Laws of refraction of light:
(i) The incident ray, the refracted ray at the point of incidence and the normal all lies in the same plane for the two given medium.
(ii) The ratio of sine of angle of incidence (i.e., $\sin i$ ) to the sine of angle of refraction (i.e., $\sin r$ ) is always constant for the light of given colour and for the given pair of media.
Mathematically, $\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=$ constant $={ }_{1} n_{2}$
The constant ' $n$ ' is called refractive index of the second medium with respect to the first medium.

Absolute refractive index of the medium is given by
$n=\frac{\text { Speed of light in vacuum (c) }}{\text { Speed of light in media (v) }}$
(b) Given $n_{\mathrm{A}}=2.0$ and $n_{\mathrm{B}}=1.5$

Speed of light in medium B $=2 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$n_{B}=\frac{\text { Speed of light in vacuum (c) }}{\text { Speed of light in media } B}$
$1.5=\frac{\mathrm{c}}{2 \times 10^{8}}$
Speed of light in vacuum $\mathrm{c}=2 \times 10^{8} \times 1.5$

$$
=3.0 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
$$

Speed of light in medium ' $A$ '
$n_{A}=\frac{\text { Speed of light in vacuum }}{\text { Speed of light in media ' } A \text { ' }}$
$2.0=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{\text { Speed of light in media' } A^{\prime}}$
Speed of light in medium $A=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2}$

$$
=1.5 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
$$

9. (i) Optical centre: It is a point on the principal axis of the lens, such that a ray of light passing through it goes undeviated.
(ii) $f=-20 \mathrm{~cm}$
$h 1=4 \mathrm{~cm}, v=-10, u=? h 2=?$
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}$
$\frac{1}{u}=\frac{-1}{10}+\frac{1}{20} \Rightarrow \frac{-2+1}{20}=-\frac{1}{20} \Rightarrow u=-20 \mathrm{~cm}$
$h_{i}=\frac{v}{u} h 0=\frac{-10 \mathrm{~cm}}{-20 \mathrm{~cm}} \times 4=2 \mathrm{~cm}$
size of image is 2 cm .
(iii)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-066.jpg?height=269&width=556&top_left_y=1822&top_left_x=1160)
10. (i) Two light rays whose path of reflection are known are:
(a) The incident ray passes through the center of curvature: In this case, light after reflecting from the concave mirror comes back along the same path. This happens because light is incident prependicular on the mirror surface.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-066.jpg?height=257&width=371&top_left_y=2334&top_left_x=1254)
Concave mirror

## Physics

(b) The ray incident parallel to the principal axis: In this case, the incident ray will be reflected back by the reflecting surface of the concave mirror and passes through the focus ' $F$ ' of the mirror.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-067.jpg?height=270&width=389&top_left_y=466&top_left_x=443)

Let an object is place between the focus and pole of the concave mirror. The using above two rays, image of the object can be located as shown in given ray diagram.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-067.jpg?height=330&width=691&top_left_y=925&top_left_x=294)

The image is formed behind the mirror. The image is virtual, erect and magnified.
(ii) Given, $u=-20 \mathrm{~cm}, v=?, m=3$

As we know,
$m=\frac{h_{i}}{h_{0}}=\frac{-v}{u}$
As mirror produces three time magnified image thus $-3=-(v /-20) \Rightarrow v=-60 \mathrm{~cm}$
The screen is placed in front of the mirrors at a distance of 60 cm from the pole of the mirror. Thus, the screen is placed 40 cm away from the object.
11. (i) Given,

Object distance, $u=60 \mathrm{~cm}$; focal length of the lens, $f=30 \mathrm{~cm}$
Image distance, $v=$ ?
Using lens formula, $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-60}=\frac{1}{(-30)}(\because$ lens is concave $)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{-60}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{-1-2}{60}=-\frac{3}{60}=-\frac{1}{20}$
$\Rightarrow v=-20 \mathrm{~cm}$
Image distance, $v=-20 \mathrm{~cm}$
size, $m=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-20}{-60}=\frac{1}{3}$
(ii) Characteristics of the image:

Nature : Virtual
Position : 20 cm from the lens (infront of lens)

Size : Diminished $\left(\frac{1}{3}\right.$ rd of object size $)$
Erect / inverted : Erect
(iii) Ray diagram to justify answer of part (ii)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-067.jpg?height=371&width=677&top_left_y=502&top_left_x=1231)

Clearly, image formed is virtual, erect and smaller than object size.
12. (a) For security mirror (convex mirror)

Object distance $(\mathrm{u})=-20 \mathrm{~m}$
and focal length $(\mathrm{f})=$ radius of curvature $/ 2=5 / 2 =2.5 \mathrm{~m}$

Using mirror formula
$1 / \mathrm{v}+1 / \mathrm{u}=1 / \mathrm{f}$
$\Rightarrow 1 / v+1 /(-20)=1 / 2.5$
$\Rightarrow 1 / \mathrm{v}=1 / 2.5+1 / 20=9 / 20$
∴ Image distance, $\mathrm{v}=20 / 9$
Here, Image distance (v) is positive hence image formed on the other side of mirror as object.

Nature of image: Virtual and erect.
Size of the image formed in the security mirror.
Magnification, $\mathrm{m}=-\mathrm{v} / \mathrm{u}=\frac{\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{o}}}\left[\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{i}} \& \mathrm{~h}_{\mathrm{o}}=\right.$ size of image \& object respectively]

So, $\mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{i}} / \mathrm{h}_{\mathrm{o}}=(20 / 9) / 20=1 / 9$
Magnification will be $1 / 9$ th of size of customer. Hence, image should be smaller than object (customer).

Note

Convex mirror is also used as rear view mirror in vehicles because it has a wider field of view.
(b) Dentists use concave mirror because when object is placed between focus (F) and pole (P) of concave mirror then a virtual, erect and enlarged image is formed behind the mirror. It helps the doctor to see a larger image of tooth.
13. (i) Palmists use convex lens because it produces enlarged, virtual and erect image when object is placed between focus $\left(\mathrm{F}_{1}\right)$ and optical center $(\mathrm{O})$ of convex lens (see fig.)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-068.jpg?height=252&width=821&top_left_y=493&top_left_x=155)
(ii) If the palmist wants real and magnified image, he should put object between F and 2F (see in fig.). But in that case he will have to use a screen to see the image.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-068.jpg?height=202&width=830&top_left_y=900&top_left_x=153)
(iii) Given, $\mathrm{f}=10 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{u}=-5 \mathrm{~cm}$

Using lens formula: $1 / \mathrm{v}-1 / \mathrm{u}=1 / \mathrm{f}$

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & 1 / v-1 /(-5)=1 / 10 \\
\Rightarrow & 1 / v=1 / 10-1 / 5=-1 / 10
\end{array}
$$

Image is formed at 10 cm on the same side of lens (as object). It is erect and virtual.
Magnification, $\mathrm{m}=\mathrm{v} / \mathrm{u}=(-10) /(-5)=2$
i.e., Image is twice as big as object or, image is magnified two times.

## Note

Convex lens gives positive magnification for virtual image and negative magnification for real image.

## Reasoning Based Questions

1. It is because of lateral inversion or sideways reversal.
2. Sheet of paper produces diffuse or irregular reflection.
3. It is because lot of sun's heat rays are concentrated at the point of sun's image which burn the hole in carbon paper.
4. When a ray of light falls normally on the mirror surface, angle of incidence $=$ angle of reflection $=0^{\circ}$
5. Concave mirror produces inverted image of distant objects. Hence vehicles running on the road will appear upside down. Also, produces large size image, so covers less area behind the vehicle.
6. Towards the normal, as its speed decreases rarer to denser medium.
7. A beam of light bend when it enters at an angle because its speed decreases in glass. It does not bend, if it enters the glass at right angle as all the parts of light waves reach the glass slab at the same time, enter the glass slab at the same time and hence get slowed down at the same time. Hence no change in direction.
8. A real image can be formed on a screen because light rays actually pass through a real image. And a virtual image cannot be formed on a screen because light rays do not actually pass through a virtual image.
9. A convex lens of short focal length causes more bending of light rays passing through it as power of lens, $P=\frac{1}{f \text { (focal length in metre) }}$
10. Thick convex lens as it has shorter focal length and power of lens, $P=\frac{1}{f \text { (focal length in metre) }}$

## HOTS Questions

1. Using, $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$\therefore v=+20$
$\frac{h_{i}}{h_{0}}=\frac{v}{u}$
$\therefore h_{i}=\frac{v}{u} h_{0}=-5 \mathrm{~cm}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-068.jpg?height=318&width=657&top_left_y=1247&top_left_x=1096)

So, image is real, inverted and of same size as the object on the other side of the lens.
2. Since lens is convex, therefore $f$ is positive.

Given $u=-20 \mathrm{~cm}, f=+12 \mathrm{~cm}, h_{0}=3 \mathrm{~cm}, v=?, h_{i}=$ ?
Since formula for a lens is
$\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}, \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-20}=\frac{1}{12}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{20}=\frac{1}{12} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{12}-\frac{1}{20}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{5-3}{60}=\frac{1}{30} \Rightarrow v=30 \mathrm{~cm}$
Since ' $v$ ' is positive, the image is located on the other side of the lens.
$m=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{+30}{-20}=-1.5$
Since $m$ is negative and greater than 1 , the image is real, inverted and larger than the object.
$m=\frac{h_{i}}{h_{0}} \Rightarrow-1.5=\frac{h_{i}}{3}$ or $h_{i}=-4.5 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Thus the image is 30 cm from the convex lens, located on the other side of the lens. It is real, inverted and enlarged ( 4.5 cm in hight)
3. A concave lens always forms a virtual, erect image on the same side of the object.
Image distance $v=-10 \mathrm{~cm}$
Focal length $f=-15 \mathrm{~cm}$,
Object distance $u=$ ?
Since $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$ or $\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{f}$
$\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{-10}-\frac{1}{(-15)}=-\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{15}$
$\frac{1}{u}=\frac{-3+2}{30}=\frac{1}{-30}$ or $u=-30 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Thus, the object distance is 30 cm .
Magnification, $m=\frac{v}{u}$ or $m=\frac{-10 \mathrm{~cm}}{-30 \mathrm{~cm}}=\frac{1}{3}=+0.33$
4. A very small head → Convex mirror

A fat body → Concave mirror
Legs of normal size → Plane mirror
Case Based MCQs

1. (i) (a) From figure, angle of incidence, $i=60^{\circ}$ and angle of refraction, $r=45^{\circ}$
Refractive index of the medium $B$ relative to medium $A$, (from Snell's law)
$\mu_{B A}=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 45^{\circ}}=\frac{\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$
(ii) (a) Since light rays in the medium $B$ goes towards normal (figure), so it has greater refractive index i.e., denser w.r.t. medium $A$. Hence, refractive index of medium $B$ relative to medium $A$ is greater than unity.
(iii) (b) In a rectangular glass slab, the emergent rays are parallel to the direction of the incident ray, as the extent of bending of the ray of light at the opposite parallel faces air-glass and glass-air interface of the rectangular glass slab is equal and opposite.
This is why the ray emerges are parallel to the incident ray.
(iv) (d) Among the given material kerosene refractive index, $\mu=1.44$, water $\mu=1.33$, mustard oil $\mu=1.46$ and glycerine $\mu=1.74$. Glycerine is most optically denser. Therefore, ray of light bend most in glycerine.
(v) (a) $\frac{\sin \mathrm{i}}{\sin \mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{n}_{21}=\frac{\mathrm{v}_{1}}{\mathrm{v}_{2}}$

## Text - Book Questions

1. The point on principal axis where beam of rays parallel to principal axis meet after reflection from the mirror is called principal focus.
2. $R=2 f, R=20 \mathrm{~cm}$
$f=R / 2=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
3. Concave mirror can produce the erect and enlarge image of an object.
4. Because its aperture is large and it always forms virtual, erect and small image of distant objects.
5. $R=+32 \mathrm{~cm}$
$f=R / 2=+16 \mathrm{~cm}$
6. $m=-v / u$ (real)
$-3=\frac{-v}{u}$
$v=3 u, u=10 \mathrm{~cm}$ (given)
7. It will bend towards the normal because the velocity of light decreases as it enters into water (denser medium).
8. ${ }_{a} \mu_{g}=1.5 ; v=? ; c=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$
${ }_{a} \mu_{g}=\frac{c}{v} \Rightarrow v=\frac{c}{{ }_{a} \mu_{g}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1.5}=2 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}$
9. Light travels fastest in water because the refractive index of water is least as compared to kerosene and turpentines.
10. When the focal length of a lens is one metre then the power of lens is called one dioptre.
11. $v=+50 \mathrm{~cm} ; m=1 ; P=? u=?, m=-1$
$m=\frac{v}{u}, \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}, P=\frac{1}{f}$
$-1=\frac{50}{u}, \frac{1}{50}+\frac{1}{50}=\frac{1}{f}, P=\frac{1 \times 100}{25}$
$u=-50 \mathrm{~cm}, f=+25 \mathrm{~cm}, P=4$ dioptre
12. $f=-2 \mathrm{~m}$
$P=\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{-2}=-0.5$ dioptre

$\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-070.jpg?height=310&width=634&top_left_y=1315&top_left_x=223)
$\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{25}=\frac{5-2}{50}=\frac{3}{50}$
$v=\frac{50}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ and magnification, $m=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{h_{i}}{h_{0}},-\frac{50}{3 \times 25}=\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{h}_{1}=-3.33 \mathrm{~cm}$
The image is real and inverted.
22. $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$-\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{u}=-\frac{1}{15}$
$\frac{1}{u}=-\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{15}=\frac{-3+2}{30}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-070.jpg?height=305&width=496&top_left_y=1948&top_left_x=484)
$=-\frac{1}{30}$

23. $f=15 \mathrm{~cm} ; u=-10 \mathrm{~cm} ; v=$ ?
$\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{10}=\frac{2+3}{30}=\frac{5}{30}=\frac{1}{6}$
Magnification,
$m=\frac{-v}{u}$
$m=\frac{-6}{-10}=0.6$
$v=6 \mathrm{~cm}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-070.jpg?height=273&width=417&top_left_y=424&top_left_x=1370)

Image is formed on other side of mirror, so it is virtual and erect.
12. The magnification of a plane mirror is +1 , it means that the image is of same size of the object. Also, it is erect and virtual.
13. $h_{0}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$; $u=-20 \mathrm{~cm}$; $h_{i}=+30 \mathrm{~cm}$;
$f=R / 2=15 \mathrm{~cm} ; u=? ; h_{i}=$ ?
$\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{-20}=\frac{1}{15}+\frac{1}{20}=\frac{4+3}{60}=\frac{7}{60}$
$v=\frac{60}{7} \mathrm{~cm}$ Image is formed on the other side of mirror.
$m=-\frac{v}{u}=\frac{h_{i}}{h_{0}}$
$\frac{-60}{-7 \times 20}=\frac{h_{i}}{5} \Rightarrow h_{i}=\frac{3}{7} \times 5=2.175 \mathrm{~cm}$ image is virtual, erect and small
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-070.jpg?height=273&width=410&top_left_y=1493&top_left_x=1233)

$-\frac{1}{27}+\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{-18}$
$\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{-18}+\frac{1}{27}=\frac{-3+2}{54}=\frac{-1}{54}$
$v=-54 \mathrm{~cm}$ Image is formed on same side of mirror.
$\frac{h_{i}}{5}=\frac{-(-54)}{-27}=-10 \mathrm{~cm}$ real, inverted and enlarged in size
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-070.jpg?height=365&width=592&top_left_y=2256&top_left_x=1142)

## Exemplar Questions

1. No, pencil will not appear to bent to the same extent, because bending of light will be different in different liquids since velocity of light at the interface separating two media depends on the relative refractive index of the medium. In case of kerosene, it is more optically denser (refractive index) than water. So, the extent by which light bends in kerosene will be more than how much it bends in water. Hence, the pencil will appear ore bend in kerosene.
2. Sudha should move the screen towards the lens so as the obtain a clear image of the building. The approximate focal lenth of this lens will be 15 cm .

cm , image is real, enlarged and inverted. The lens is convex.

67. (b) Using lens maker's formula,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right) \\
& \frac{1}{f_{1}}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-20}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow f_{1}=40 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-071.jpg?height=323&width=323&top_left_y=740&top_left_x=1603)
$\frac{1}{f_{2}}=\left(\frac{1.7}{1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{-20}-\frac{1}{+20}\right)$
$\Rightarrow f_{2}=-\frac{100}{7} \mathrm{~cm}$
and $\frac{1}{f_{3}}=\left(\frac{1.5}{1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{-20}\right)$
$\Rightarrow f_{3}=40 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\frac{1}{f_{e q}}=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}}+\frac{1}{f_{3}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{f_{e q}}=\frac{1}{40}+\frac{1}{-100 / 7}+\frac{1}{40}$
$\therefore f_{e q}=-50 \mathrm{~cm}$
Therefore, the focal length of the combination is -50 cm .

69. (b) $P=P_{1}+P_{2}$
$\Rightarrow P=\frac{1}{f_{1}}+\frac{1}{f_{2}} \Rightarrow P=\frac{f_{1}+f_{2}}{f_{1} f_{2}}$
70. (c) According to laws of reflection, angle of incidence $=$ angle of reflection
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-071.jpg?height=629&width=653&top_left_y=1941&top_left_x=1228)
$\therefore \quad$ if a vector $\vec{r}$ is along the reflected ray, then

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \vec{r}=\cos 30^{\circ} \hat{i}-\sin 30 \hat{j} \\
& \vec{r}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \hat{i}-\frac{1}{2} \hat{j} \\
& \vec{r}=\sqrt{3} \hat{i}-\hat{j}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the direction of the reflected ray vector is . $\sqrt{3} \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}$
71. (a) Angle of incidence, $i=90^{\circ}-\theta$, decreases with increase in $\theta$ upto angle of incidence $i=$ critical angle reflection takes place so $x$ is positive and beyond the critical angle refraction takes place so $x$ is negative. Hence graph 'A' correctly depicts variation of $x$ with the angle $\theta$.
72. (c) For lens $\mathrm{A}, \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{v}-\left(-\frac{1}{40}\right) \stackrel{\lceil }{O}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{40} \longleftrightarrow 40 \mathrm{~cm} \longrightarrow \bigoplus_{30 \mathrm{~cm} \longrightarrow}^{A} =\frac{4-3}{120}=\frac{1}{120}$ or, $v=120 \mathrm{~cm}$.

For lens $\mathrm{B}, u=90 \mathrm{~cm}[u=120-30]$
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{90}$
$\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{30}+\frac{1}{90}=\frac{3+1}{90}$
or, $v=22.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Which is positive so that it is 22.5 cm from lens B.
73. (d) Two mirrors are inclined at an angle, $\theta=$ ?

According to question, emergent ray is parallel to incident ray
∴ deviation angle $\delta=180^{\circ}$
But $\delta=360^{\circ}-2 \theta$
or, $360^{\circ}-2 \theta=180^{\circ}$
or, $2 \theta=180^{\circ}$
$\therefore \theta=90^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-072.jpg?height=323&width=323&top_left_y=2014&top_left_x=662)
74. (b)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-072.jpg?height=259&width=407&top_left_y=2330&top_left_x=290)

Using mirror formula, $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}+\frac{1}{u}$
$-\frac{1}{15}=\frac{1}{v_{1}}+\frac{1}{u} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v_{1}}=\frac{1}{-15}+\frac{1}{40}$
$\therefore \quad v_{1}=-24 \mathrm{~cm}$
When object is displaced by 20 cm towards mirror
Now, $\quad \mathrm{u}_{2}=-20$
So, $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}+\frac{1}{u_{2}}$
$\frac{1}{-15}=\frac{1}{v_{2}}-\frac{1}{20} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v_{2}}=\frac{1}{20}-\frac{1}{15}$
$\therefore v_{2}=-60 \mathrm{~cm}$

Therefore image shifts away from mirror by $=60-24=36 \mathrm{~cm}$
75. (c) For the end $B$, image distance of end $B$ will be,
$f=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
$u_{B}=-18 \mathrm{~cm}$
$v_{B}=$ image distance of end $B$
As we know,
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v_{B}}-\frac{1}{u_{B}}$
$\frac{1}{v_{B}}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{u_{B}}$
$\frac{1}{v_{B}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{18}=\frac{8}{180}$
$v_{B}=\frac{180}{8} \Rightarrow 22.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Similarly, for the end $A$, image distance of end $A$ will be,
$f=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
$u_{A}=-20 \mathrm{~cm}$
$v_{A}=$ image distance of end $A$
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v_{A}}-\frac{1}{u_{A}}$
$\frac{1}{v_{A}}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{u_{A}}$
$\frac{1}{v_{A}}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{20}=\frac{1}{20}$
$v_{A}=20 \mathrm{~cm}$
So, length of image $A^{\prime} B^{\prime}=\left(v_{B}-v_{A}\right)$
$=22.5-20=2.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
So magnification, $m=\frac{A^{\prime} B^{\prime}}{A B} \Rightarrow \frac{2.5}{2}=1.25$

using lens formula

\begin{aligned}

80. (b) Light ray emerges normally from another surface, hence, $e$ (angle of emergence) $=0$
$\therefore r_{2}=0 \quad r_{1}+r_{2}=A \Rightarrow r_{1}=A$
$\mu_{1} \cdot \sin i=\mu_{2} \cdot \sin r$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-073.jpg?height=298&width=540&top_left_y=2017&top_left_x=349)

Applying Snell's law on first surface $P Q$
$\Rightarrow \mu_{1} \cdot \sin i=\mu_{2} \cdot \sin r_{1} \Rightarrow \sin i=\mu \sin A$
For small angles ( $\sin \theta \approx \theta$ )
$\therefore \quad i=\mu A$
81. (d) Given, using lens maker's formula

$$
\frac{1}{f}=(k-1)\left(\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right)
$$

Here, $R_{1}=R_{2}=R$ (For double convex lens)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{-R}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow P=\frac{1}{f}=(\mu-1) \frac{2}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

For plano convex lens,
$R_{1}=R^{\prime}, R_{2}=\infty$
Using lens maker's formula again, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1.5 P=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} P=\frac{\mu-1}{R^{\prime}}
\end{aligned}
$$

From (i) and (ii),

$$
\frac{3}{2}=\frac{R^{\prime}}{2 R} \Rightarrow R^{\prime}=\frac{R}{3}
$$

## Integer/Numeric Questions

1. (1.22)

Here, sine of angle of incidence $(\sin i)=0.866$
and sine of angle of refraction $(\sin r)=0.707$
Now, we have $\mu=\frac{\sin i}{\sin r}=\frac{0.866}{0.707}=1.22$
Hence, the refractive index of the medium is 1.22 .
2. (10)

Since the image is real and diminshed, the lens must be convex and the object must be placed beyond $2 F$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-074.jpg?height=314&width=769&top_left_y=310&top_left_x=223)

Given : $v=+18 \mathrm{~cm}$,
$\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}=\frac{-4}{5}, f=?\left[\because m=\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}\right.$ is negative for real image $]$
We have, $m=\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}=\frac{v}{u}$
$\therefore \frac{v}{u}=\frac{-4}{5} \quad$ or $\quad 4 u=-5 v \quad$ or $\quad 4 u=-5 \times 18 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore u=-22.5 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Now focal length is given by :
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{18}-\frac{1}{-22.5}=\frac{1}{18}+\frac{1}{22.5}=\frac{9}{90}$
$\therefore f=10 \mathrm{~cm}$.
3. (a) 7.5 cm
(b) 48 cm
4. (7.14)
$h_{0}=50 \mathrm{~cm}, h_{1}=-20 \mathrm{~cm}, v=10 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Using $m=\frac{h_{1}}{h_{0}}=\frac{v}{u}$, we get
With sign convention,

$$
u=\mathrm{v} \frac{h_{0}}{h_{1}}=10 \times \frac{50}{(-20)}=-25 \mathrm{~cm}
$$

Using $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}$ we get,
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{-25}=\frac{25+10}{250}$
$f=\frac{250}{35}=7.14 \mathrm{~cm}$.
5. (4)
$h_{0}=20 \mathrm{~cm}, h_{1}=-30 \mathrm{~cm}, v=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
$m=\frac{h_{1}}{h_{0}}=\frac{(-30)}{20}=\frac{v}{u}$
$\therefore u=-\frac{10 \times 2}{3}=-\frac{20}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$.

Using $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}$ we get,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{10}-\frac{1}{\left(-\frac{20}{3}\right)}=\frac{1}{10}+\frac{3}{20}=\frac{5}{20}=\frac{1}{4} \\
& f=+4 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

6. (11.25)
$h_{0}=30 \mathrm{~cm}, h_{1}=-10 \mathrm{~cm}, v=15 \mathrm{~cm}$
Using $m=\frac{h_{1}}{h_{0}}=\frac{v}{u}$, we get
$\therefore \mathrm{u}=v \frac{h_{1}}{h_{0}}=15 \times \frac{30}{-10}=-45 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Using $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}$ we get,
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{-45}=\frac{3+1}{45}=\frac{4}{45}$
$f=\frac{4}{45}=11.25 \mathrm{~cm}$.
7. $(-60)$

Given : For convex lens
$f=+30 \mathrm{~cm}, u=?, v=+60 \mathrm{~cm}$,
From lens formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u} \therefore \frac{1}{30}=\frac{1}{60}-\frac{1}{u} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{60}-\frac{1}{30}=\frac{1-2}{60} \\
& \frac{1}{u}=-\frac{1}{60} \quad \therefore u=-60 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

So, the object should be placed at a distance of 60 cm to the left of the lens.
8. $(-30,2)$
$f=+20 \mathrm{~cm}$, Since image is real, $m=-2$
Using, $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u} \quad$ and $m=\frac{v}{u}$
we get, $m=\frac{f}{f+u} \Rightarrow-2=\frac{20}{20+u}$
$-40-2 u=20 \Rightarrow-2 u=60 \Rightarrow u=-30 \mathrm{~cm}$.
9. $(6,3)$

Let the scale be $5 \mathrm{~cm}=1 \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ Size of the object $=5 \mathrm{~cm}=1 \mathrm{~cm}$
∴ Distance of the object from the lens $=10 \mathrm{~cm}=2 \mathrm{~cm}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-075.jpg?height=332&width=743&top_left_y=310&top_left_x=306)
∴ Focal length of the lens $=15 \mathrm{~cm}=3 \mathrm{~cm}$
On constructing the ray diagram, it is found
∴ Size of the image $=A_{1} B_{1}=0.6 \mathrm{~cm}=0.6 \times 5 \mathrm{~cm}=3.0 \mathrm{~cm}$
Distance of the image from lens $=O B_{1}=1.2 \mathrm{~cm}=1.2 \times 5 \mathrm{~cm}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$
Nature : Image is virtual, erect and diminished
10. $(30,0.33)$

Here $h=3.0 \mathrm{~cm}, f=-15 \mathrm{~cm}, v=-10.0 \mathrm{~cm}$
(i) From lens formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{f} & =\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u} \\
\therefore \frac{1}{u} & =\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{-10}-\frac{1}{-15} \\
\frac{1}{u} & =\frac{1}{15}-\frac{1}{10}=-\frac{1}{30} \quad \therefore \quad u=-30 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) (I) $m=\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}=\frac{v}{u}=\frac{-10}{-30}$
$\therefore h^{\prime}=+\frac{1}{3}=0.33$
From (i) and (ii), it is clear that image is formed on the same side of object placed at a distance of 30 cm and image is virtual, erect.
11. $(20,2)$

For a concave mirror
Given : $h=2 \mathrm{~cm}, \mathrm{u}=-20 \mathrm{~cm}, f=-10 \mathrm{~cm}$

$$
v=? h^{\prime}=?
$$

From mirror equation, we know that

$$
\begin{gathered}
\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{-10}-\frac{1}{-20}=-\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{20} \\
=\frac{-2+1}{20}=\frac{-1}{20} \\
\therefore \quad v=-20 \mathrm{~cm} .
\end{gathered}
$$

Also $m=\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}=-\frac{v}{u} \Rightarrow h^{\prime}=-\frac{(-20) \times 2}{-20}=-2$
$\therefore \quad$ Image size $h^{\prime}=-2 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Position of image is 20 cm on the same side of object.
Nature of image : Real, inverted, same size ( $h^{\prime}=h$ ).
12. $(-37.5)$

Focal length of convex lens, $f=+25 \mathrm{~cm}$ image distance, $v=+75 \mathrm{~cm}$
From lens formula
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u} \quad \therefore \frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{75}=\frac{1}{25}=-\frac{2}{75}$
$\Rightarrow u=-\frac{75}{2}=-37.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
The image is real.
13. $(18,6)$

For a concave mirror,
$h=+3 \mathrm{~cm}, u=-9 \mathrm{~cm}, f=-18 \mathrm{~cm}, v=?, h^{\prime}=$ ?
From mirror equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u} \quad \therefore \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{-18}-\frac{1}{-9}=-\frac{1}{18}+\frac{1}{9}=\frac{1}{18} \quad \therefore v=+18 \mathrm{~cm} .
\end{aligned}
$$

i.e., image is formed behind the mirror at a distance of 18 cm . It is virtual.
Also, magnification $m=\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}=-\frac{v}{u}$
$\therefore \frac{h^{\prime}}{+3}=-\frac{+18}{-9}=+2 \quad \therefore h^{\prime}=+6 \mathrm{~cm}$
So, image is erect and twice the size of object.
14. (6)

Here $\mathrm{u}=-30 \mathrm{~cm}, m=\frac{1}{6}$
$m=\frac{-v}{u} \Rightarrow \frac{-v}{(-30)}=\frac{1}{6} \Rightarrow v=5 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{5}+\frac{1}{(-30)}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow f=6 \mathrm{~cm}$
15. $(12,6)$
$h_{0}=3 \mathrm{~cm}, u=-6 \mathrm{~cm}, f=-12 \mathrm{~cm}$
Mirror formula : $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}$

$$
\frac{1}{-12}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-6}
$$

$\frac{1}{v}=\frac{-1}{12}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{-1+2}{12} ; \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{12}$
$\therefore \quad v=+12 \mathrm{~cm}$
$m=\frac{h^{\prime}}{h}=-\frac{v}{u}$
$h^{\prime}=-\frac{v}{u} \times h=\frac{-12}{-6} \times 3=+6$
Position : image is formed behind the mirror at a distance of 12 cm .
Nature : virtual, erect, enlarged
Size of image : Twice the size of object.
16. $(-10)$
$f=-20 \mathrm{~cm}$. Since image is virtual $m=+2$.
Using $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}$ and $m=-\frac{v}{u}$
we get $m=\frac{f}{f-u} \quad \therefore 2=\frac{-20}{-20-u}$
$-40-2 u=-20 \Rightarrow 2 u=-20 \Rightarrow u=-10 \mathrm{~cm}$.
17. $(10,20)$
$f=-15 \mathrm{~cm}, h_{1}=3 h_{0}$
For Real Image,

$$
m=-\frac{v}{u}=-3 \quad \therefore v=3 u
$$

using $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}$, we get, $\frac{1}{-15}=\frac{1}{3 u}+\frac{1}{u} \Rightarrow u=-20$ cm .
For Virtual Image,

$$
m=-\frac{v}{u}=+3 \Rightarrow v=-3 u
$$

using $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}$
we get, $\frac{1}{-15}=\frac{1}{(-3 u)}+\frac{1}{u} \Rightarrow u=-10 \mathrm{~cm}$.
The two possible positions are 10 cm and 20 cm in front of concave mirror.
18. $\lambda_{\text {air }}=589 n m=589 \times 10^{-9} m$

Refractive index of water $=\mu=1.33$
(a) $(589 \mathrm{~nm})\left(5.09 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}, 3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)$ Reflected light will have same wavelength, frequency and velocity.
$\therefore \lambda=589 n m, c=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f=\frac{c}{\lambda}= & \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{589 \times 10^{-9}}=5.09 \times 10^{-3+8+9} \\
& =5.09 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

(b) ( $442 \mathrm{~nm}, 5.09 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz}, 2.25 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )The frequency is same with refracted light. But, velocity and wavelength vary.
Since, $\mu=\frac{c}{v}$,
velocity in water $=\frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1.33}=2.25 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
Since, $\mu=\frac{\lambda}{\lambda^{\prime}}$,
wavelength in water is $\lambda^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda}{\mu}=\frac{589 \times 10^{-9}}{1.33}=442 \mathrm{~nm}$.
19. $(30,-20)$
$h_{0}=10 \mathrm{~cm}, \quad u=-15 \mathrm{~cm}, f=+10 \mathrm{~cm}$
using $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}$, we get,
$\frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{-15}=\frac{15-10}{150}=\frac{5}{150}$
$v=\frac{150}{5}=30 \mathrm{~cm}$
Image formed is real and is at a distance of 30 cm .
$\frac{h_{1}}{h_{0}}=\frac{v}{u}=\therefore h_{1}=\frac{30}{-15} \times 10=-20$
Image is inverted since $\mathrm{h}_{1}$ is $-v e$.
20. $(30,3)$

For a concave mirror

Given : $f=-15 \mathrm{~cm}, u=-10 \mathrm{~cm}, v=?, \quad m=$ ?
From mirror equation
$\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore & \frac{1}{v}=\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{-15}-\frac{1}{-10}=\frac{-1}{15}+\frac{1}{10} \\
& \frac{-10+15}{150}=+\frac{5}{150}=+\frac{1}{30} \therefore v=+30 \mathrm{~cm} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Also, Magnification, $m=-\frac{v}{u}=-\frac{+30}{-10}=3$.
Hence, the image is formed at a distance of 30 cm behind the mirror.
Nature of image : Virtual, erect.
Size of image : Magnified, i.e., thrice the size of object.

6. (d) $i_{B}>i_{A}$ or $\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\mu_{B}}\right)>\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\mu_{A}}\right)$ or $\mu_{A}>\mu_{B}$

Hence medium $A$ is denser and $B$ is rarer.
Total internal Reflection : When the object is placed in a optically denser medium and if the incident angle is greater than the critical angle then the ray of light gets reflected back to the originating medium. For critical angle ( $\alpha$ ) from medium $A$ to $B,{ }_{B} \mu_{A}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& { }_{B} \mu_{A}=\frac{1}{\sin \alpha} \text { or } \sin \alpha=\frac{1}{{ }_{B} \mu_{A}}=\frac{\mu_{B}}{\mu_{A}} \\
& \Rightarrow \sin \alpha=\frac{1}{\sin i_{B}} \times \frac{\sin i_{A}}{1} \\
& \Rightarrow \alpha=\sin ^{-1}\left[\frac{\sin i_{A}}{\sin i_{B}}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

7. (d) The waves reflected from the top layer of oil interfere with the wave train reflected from the lower surface of thin oil film producing light and dark coloured pattern.
8. (a) The critical angle for total internal reflection from glass to water,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sin i_{c}=\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{water}}}{\mu_{\mathrm{glass}}}=\frac{1.33}{1.50}=\frac{8}{9} . \\
& i_{c}=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{8}{9}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

9. (a) 10. (b)
10. (a) Initially three convex lenses of 10 cm focal length each placed at equal spacing of 30 cm and one end of combined system is at a distance of 10 cm from a point source.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-077.jpg?height=302&width=550&top_left_y=315&top_left_x=360)

In second case, when device is moved away another 10 cm i.e., 20 cm from source $O$, then
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-077.jpg?height=256&width=552&top_left_y=788&top_left_x=365)

The distance between object and image in both cases is 90 cm because of principle of reversibility. Hence, there is no shift in image's position.
12. (b) Angle of prism $A=180^{\circ}-80^{\circ}=100^{\circ}$
( ∵ Base angle $=40^{\circ}$ )
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-077.jpg?height=333&width=543&top_left_y=1347&top_left_x=360)

For TIR, $i>\theta_{c} \Rightarrow \sin 40^{\circ}>\sin \theta_{c}$
$\Rightarrow \sin 40^{\circ}>\frac{\mu_{w}}{\mu_{g}} \Rightarrow \mu_{g}>\frac{1.33}{\sin 40^{\circ}} \Rightarrow 2.07$
or $\mu_{g}>2.07$
13. (d) Using, mirror formula,

$$
\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}
$$

Here, $f=+10 \mathrm{~cm}, u=-15 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\Rightarrow v=\frac{10 \times-15}{-15+10}=30 \mathrm{~cm}$
To get speed of image, differentiation Eq. (i) with respect to time,
$\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{v^{2}}{u^{2}}\left(\frac{d u}{d t}\right)$
$\therefore \frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{(+30)^{2}}{(-15)^{2}} \times\left(+2 \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d t}=+8 \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$
So, image is moving at $8 \mathrm{~cm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ away from lens.
14. (d) Here $\underset{(1.6)}{\mu_{c s_{2}}}>\underset{(1.33)}{\mu_{\text {water }}}>\underset{(1.0)}{\mu_{\text {air }}}$
i.e., $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$ is denser than water and water is denser than air. When medium outside a lens is denser than medium of lens, then a concave lens will acts like a convex lens and vice-versa.
Hence, lens here acts as a diverging lens when filled with $\mathrm{CS}_{2}$ and immersed in water.
15. (c) As parallel beam incident on diverging lens will form image at focus.
$\therefore v=-25 \mathrm{~cm}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-077.jpg?height=387&width=508&top_left_y=813&top_left_x=1235)
$f=-25 c m$
$f=20 \mathrm{~cm}$
The image formed by diverging lens is used as an object for converging lens,
So for converging lens $u=-25-15=-40 c m, f= 20 c m$
∴ Final image formed by converging lens
$\frac{1}{V}-\frac{1}{-40}=\frac{1}{20}$
or, $V=40 \mathrm{~cm}$ from converging lens real and inverted.
16. (d) When mirror is rotated by angle $\theta$ reflected ray will be rotated by $2 \theta$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-077.jpg?height=309&width=483&top_left_y=1795&top_left_x=1233)
$\frac{y}{x}=2 \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\frac{y}{2 x}$
17. (b) Given: $d_{1}=5 \mathrm{~cm}, \mu_{1}=1.33$

$$
d_{2}=2 \mathrm{~cm}, \mu_{2}=1.5
$$

$d_{1}$ and $d_{2}$ are the thickness of slabs of medium with refractive index $\mu_{1}$ and $\mu_{2}$, respectively.
using formula, $d=\frac{d_{1}}{\mu_{1}}+\frac{d_{2}}{\mu_{2}}+\ldots .$.

Apparent depth, $d=\frac{5}{1.33}+\frac{2}{1.5} =5.088 \mathrm{~cm}=5.1 \mathrm{~cm}$
18. (d)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-078.jpg?height=250&width=419&top_left_y=456&top_left_x=328)

As the object and image distance is same, object is placed at $2 f$. Therefore $2 f=10$
or $f=5 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Shift due to slab, $d=t\left(1-\frac{1}{\mu}\right)$
in the direction of incident ray
$\Rightarrow d=1.5\left(1-\frac{2}{3}\right)=0.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Now, $u=-9.5 \mathrm{~cm}$
Again using lens formulas $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-9.5}=\frac{1}{5}$
$\Rightarrow v=10.55 \mathrm{~cm}$
Thus, screen is shifted by a distance $d=10.55-10 =0.55 \mathrm{~cm}$ away from the lens.
19. (b)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-078.jpg?height=321&width=478&top_left_y=1576&top_left_x=283)

Equivalent focal length in air

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{F_{1}}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{f}=\frac{2}{f} \\
& \Rightarrow F_{1}=\frac{f}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

When glycerin is filled inside, it behaves like a concave lens of focal length $(-f)$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-078.jpg?height=325&width=506&top_left_y=2266&top_left_x=287)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad \frac{1}{F_{2}}=\frac{1}{f}+\frac{1}{f}-\frac{1}{f} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad F_{2}=\mathrm{f}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing (i) by (ii), we get

$$
\frac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}=\frac{1}{2}
$$

23. (d)

Sol. 24-26
24. (a) 25. (c) 26. (b)

For lens, applying lens formula
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-078.jpg?height=248&width=506&top_left_y=1027&top_left_x=1187)
$\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-30}=\frac{1}{f}$
$\frac{1}{60}-\frac{1}{-30}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{60}+\frac{1}{30}$
$\therefore f=20 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\because \mathrm{m}=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{\mathrm{u}} \Rightarrow-2=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{-30} \Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=60 \mathrm{~cm}$
For reflection, applying mirror formula,
$\frac{1}{\mathrm{v}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{10}+\frac{1}{-30}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=30 \mathrm{~cm}$
The image formed by convex side is faint erect and virtual.
By lens maker formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\ell}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{s}}}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}\right) \\
& \therefore \frac{1}{20}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}_{l}}{1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{30}\right) \therefore \mathrm{n}_{\ell}=25
\end{aligned}
$$

27. (c) 28. (c) 29. (b)
28. (120) $\because \frac{1}{f}=\left[\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right](\mu-1)$
$\therefore \frac{1}{30}=(1.5-1)\left[\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right]$
Refractive index of glass with respect to water
$\mu_{w g}=\frac{\mu_{a g}}{\mu_{a w}}=\frac{1.5}{4} \times 3=1.125$
$\because \frac{1}{f^{\prime}}=\left(\mu_{\mathrm{wg}}-1\right)\left[\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right]$
or $\frac{1}{f^{\prime}}=[1.125-1]\left[\frac{1}{R_{1}}-\frac{1}{R_{2}}\right]$ or $\frac{f^{\prime}}{30}=\frac{0.50}{0.125}$
$\therefore f^{\prime}=120 \mathrm{~cm}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& u=-(P O) \\
& =-3 \mathrm{~cm}

$$

By refraction formula,
$\frac{\mu_{2}}{v}-\frac{\mu_{1}}{u}=\frac{\mu_{2}-\mu_{1}}{R}$, we have

$\mu_{1}=1.5, \mu_{2}=1$, and $R=-5 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\therefore \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1.5}{-3}=\frac{1-1.5}{-5}$

32. (30) For concave lens,

$$

and ![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-079.jpg?height=24&width=60&top_left_y=1158&top_left_x=386)

33. (8.75) For concave mirror, $u=-5 \mathrm{~cm}, f=-20 \mathrm{~m}$

By mirror formula, $\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$, we have
$\frac{1}{v}+\frac{1}{-5}=\frac{1}{-20}$
$\therefore v=+\frac{20}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$

Thus $O I=\frac{20}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
The distance $P I^{\prime}=\underline{20}$
$=11.67 \mathrm{~cm}$.
Now for the refraction through water surface, we have
$\mu=\frac{R D}{A D}$
$\therefore \mathrm{AD}=\frac{R D}{\mu}=\frac{11.67}{4 / 3}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-079.jpg?height=544&width=358&top_left_y=402&top_left_x=1571)
or $P I_{2}=8.75 \mathrm{~cm}$.
34. (1.15) Refractive index, $\mu=\frac{1}{\sin c}=\frac{1}{\sin 60^{\circ}}$

$$
=\frac{1}{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}=\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}=1.15
$$

35. (15) $\frac{1}{f}=\left(\frac{\mu_{2}}{\mu_{1}}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{r_{1}}-\frac{1}{r_{2}}\right)$

Using proper sign we get, $f=-15 \mathrm{~cm}$ and it behaves as concave lens.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-080.jpg?height=2296&width=1714&top_left_y=322&top_left_x=146)

## THE HUMAN EYE

The human eye is the organ which gives us the sense of sight, allowing us to learn more about the surrounding world than we do with any of the other four senses. We use our eyes in almost every activity we perform, whether reading, working, watching television, writing a letter, driving a car, and in countless other ways. Most people probably would agree that sight is the sense they value more than all the rest.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-081.jpg?height=604&width=791&top_left_y=532&top_left_x=678)
Fig. 2.1 Human eye

The eye allows us to see and interpret the shapes, colors, and dimensions of objects in the world by processing the light they reflect or emit. The eye is able to see in bright light or in dim light, but it cannot see objects when light is absent.
The diameter of human eye is about 2.3 cm .
The structure and function of its important parts are described below.
(i) Retina : It is a light sensitive screen inside the eye on which image is formed. It contains rods and cones.
(ii) Cornea : It is a thin membrane which covers the eye ball. It acts like a lens which refracts the light entering the eye.
(iii) Aqueous humour : It is fluid which fills the space between cornea and eye lens.
(iv) Eye lens : It is a Convex lens made of transparent and flexible jelly like material. Its curvature can be adjusted with the help of ciliary muscles.
(v) Pupil : It is a hole in the middle of iris through which light enters the eye. It appears black because light falling on it goes into the eye and does not come back.
(vi) Ciliary muscles : These are the muscles which are attached to eye lens and can modify the shape of eye lens which leads to the variation in focal lengths.
(vii) Iris : It controls the amount of light entering the eye by changing the size of pupil.
(viii) Optical nerve : These are the nerves which take the image to the brain in the form of electrical signals.

## WORKING OF THE HUMAN EYE

Light waves from an object (such as a tree) enter the eye first through the cornea, which is the clear dome at the front of the eye. The light then progresses through the pupil, the circular opening in the center of the coloured iris. Next, the light passes through the crystalline lens, which is located immediately behind the iris and the pupil.
Initially, the light waves are bent or converged first by the cornea, and then further by the crystalline lens, to a nodal point (N) located immediately behind the back surface of the lens. At that point, the image becomes reversed (turned backwards) and inverted (turned upside-down). Actually image of the object on the retina is real, inverted and highly diminished.
The light continues through the vitreous humor, the clear gel that makes up about $80 \%$ of the eye's volume, and then, ideally, back to a clear focus on the retina behind the vitreous body. The small central area of the retina is the macula, which provides the best vision of any location in the retina. If the eye is considered to be a type of camera, the retina is equivalent to the film inside of the camera, registering the tiny photons of light which interact with it.
Within the layers of the retina, light impulses are changed into electrical signals and then sent through the optic nerve, along the visual pathway, to the cortex at the posterior or back of the brain. Here, the electrical signals are interpreted or "seen" by the brain as a visual image. When the light entering the eyes is bright enough, the pupils will constrict (get smaller), due to light response.

Actually, then, we do not "see" with our eyes but, rather, with our brains. Our eyes merely are the beginnings of the visual process. You might have experienced that you are not able to see objects clearly for some time when you enter from bright light to a room with dim light. After sometime, however, you may be able to see things in the dim-lit room. The pupil of an eye acts like a variable aperture whose size can be varied with the help of the iris.
When the light is very bright, the iris contracts the pupil to allow less light to enter the eye. However, in dim light the iris expands the pupil to allow more light to enter the eye. Thus, the pupil opens completely through the relaxation of the iris.

## POWER OF ACCOMMODATION

The ability of the eye lens to change its shape to focus near and distant objects clearly is called power of accommodation. The table shows how this is done.

| Object | Ciliary muscles | Suspensory ligaments | Muscle tension on lens | Lens shape |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Near | Contract | Slackened | Low | Thick |
| Distant | Relax | Stretched | High | Thin |

When the eye is relaxed and the interior lens is the least rounded, the lens has its maximum focal length for distant viewing. As the muscle tension around the ring of muscle is increased and the supporting fibres are thereby loosened, the interior lens rounds out to its minimum focal length. The minimum distance, at which objects can be seen most distinctly without strain, is called the least distance of distinct vision. It is also called the near point (N.P.) of the eye. For a young adult with normal vision, the near point is about $\mathbf{2 5 ~ c m}$.
The farthest point upto which the eye can see objects clearly is called the far point (F.P.) of the eye. It is infinity for a normal eye. Thus a normal human eye can see objects clearly that are between 25 cm and infinity.
If an eye has the ability to assume a focal length of 1.80 cm (56 diopters) to view objects many miles away as well as the ability to assume a 1.68 cm focal
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-082.jpg?height=511&width=620&top_left_y=954&top_left_x=1242)
length to view an object 0.25 meters away ( 60 diopters), then its Power of Accommodation would be measured as 4 diopters (60 diopters - 56 diopters).
The healthy eye of a young adult has a power of accommodation of approximately 4 diopters. As a person grows older, the Power of Accommodation typically decreases as a person becomes less able to view nearby objects. This failure to view nearby objects leads to the need for corrective lenses.
Many animals can see clearly both in water and on land. Some have extraordinary accommodation ranges, and others have developed other strategies. Cormorants and dippers can vary the refractive power of their lenses by $40-50$ diopters, compared to about 16 diopters for an average adolescent human. The increased accommodation is due largely to highly developed sphincter muscles which vary the curvature of the front of the lens. Turtles and otters also have very strong sphincter muscles. Variations in lens geometries are used in various species of birds and fish.
Persistence of vision: The image of any object seen persists on the retina for $\frac{1}{10}$ second even after the removal of the object. This continuance of sensation of eye is called persistence of vision. This property is used in cinematography.

## DEFECTS OF VISION AND THEIR CORRECTION

## Myopia or Short-Sightedness

If the eyeball is too long or the lens too spherical, the image of distant objects is brought to a focus in front of the retina. Nearby objects can be seen more easily.
A person with myopic eye can see nearby objects clearly but cannot see far off objects distinctly. Eyeglass with concave lens corrects this problem by diverging the light rays before they enter the eye. Myopia most commonly develops in childhood or early teens (between 8 and 14). The risk of developing myopia is increased if there is a family history of it. There may also be a link between myopia and prolonged close-up work, such as reading or sitting close temporary short-sightedness, called pseudomyopia, can be caused by a number of diseases or certain drugs. For example, myopia may be the first sign of type-2 (non insulin-dependent) diabetes. Symptoms of pseudomyopia usually clear up if the underlying cause is treated to the television, although there is little scientific evidence for this.

Remedy: This defect can be corrected by using a concave lens of suitable focal length. A concave lens diverges the rays coming from the object so that they get focused at the retina.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-083.jpg?height=310&width=1666&top_left_y=399&top_left_x=242)
Fig. 2.4

Let a person can see an object at a maximum distance $x m$ (i.e. far point is $x$ ). Thus, if the eye is to see a distant object clearly, the concave lens should form the virtual image of this distant object at a distance $x$. Thus, the required focal length of the lens is $f=-x$ and power of lens $P=\frac{1}{f}=-\frac{1}{x}$.

## Learn More

Let a nearsighted person can see upto a maximum distance $x$ and wants to see upto a distance $y$ then power of corrective lens

$$
P=\frac{x-y}{x y}
$$

Clearly from figure $\quad u=-y ; \quad v=-x ; \quad f=$ ?

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{-x}-\left(-\frac{1}{y}\right)=\frac{1}{y}-\frac{1}{x}=\frac{x-y}{x y} \\
& \therefore f=\frac{x y}{x-y} \text { and } P=\frac{x-y}{x y}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-083.jpg?height=230&width=457&top_left_y=1250&top_left_x=939)
Fig. 2.5 (a)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-083.jpg?height=254&width=435&top_left_y=1238&top_left_x=1398)
Fig. 2.5 (b)

## Hypermetropia or Farsightedness

If the eyeball is too short or the lens too flat or inflexible, the light rays entering the eye-particularly those from nearby-objects will not be brought to a focus by the time they strike the retina. The image of nearby object is formed behind the retina.
A person with hypermetropic eye can see far off objects clearly but cannot see nearby objects clearly.
Remedy: Eyeglass with convex lens is used to rectify this problem. Squinting, eye rubbing, lack of interest in school, and difficulty in reading are often seen in children with hypermetropia.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-083.jpg?height=356&width=1744&top_left_y=1991&top_left_x=203)
Fig. 2.6

## Learn More

Let the farsighted eye can see a minimum distance $y$ (i.e. near point of the eye). If the eye is to see clearly an object at the least distance of distinct vision (i.e. at 25 cm ), then the image of the object should form at a distance $y$.
$\therefore \quad u=-25 \mathrm{~cm} ; v=-y ; f=$ ?
By lens formula, $\frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{-y}-\left(-\frac{1}{25 \mathrm{~cm}}\right) ; \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{25 c m}-\frac{1}{y}$
∴ Power of lens, $P=\frac{1}{25 \mathrm{~cm}}-\frac{1}{y}$

## Presbyopia ("After 40" vision)

After age 40, and most noticeably after age 45, the human eye is affected by presbyopia, which results in greater difficulty in maintaining a clear focus at a near distance with an eye which sees clearly at a far away distance. This is due to a lessening of flexibility of the crystalline lens, as well as to a weakening of the ciliary muscles which control lens focusing, both attributable to the ageing process. Presbyopia defect is corrected in the same way as hypermetropia by using spectacles having convex lenses. It is also possible that same person has both the defects of vision myopia as well as hypermetropia. In such case, one often requires bifocal lenses. A common type of bi-focal lenses consists of both concave and convex lenses. The upper portion consists of a concave lens. It facilitates distant vision. The lower part is a convex lens. It facilitates near vision. These days, it is possible to correct the refractive defects of eyes with contact lenses or through surgical interventions.

## Astigmatism

Astigmatism is the most common refractive problem responsible for blurry vision. Most of the eyeball's focusing power occurs along the front surface of the eye, involving the tear film and cornea (the clear 'window' along the front of the eyeball).
The ideal cornea has a perfectly the round surface.Any thing other than perfectly round contributes to abnormal connect. Curvature this is a stigmatism. It also occurs due to imperfection in the curvature of the lens inside the eye. Here's a good way to demonstrate the effects of astigmatism, look at your reflection in the curved surface of a round soup spoon and compare it with your reflection. The cornea is the transparent layer over the coloured part of the eye. It blends (refracts) light rays and helps focus the figures onto the retina in back of the eye so people can see. When
the cornea is oblong shaped, it causes light rays to focus on two different points on the retina, instead of just one. As a result, people with significant astigmatism may have distorted or blurry vision.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-084.jpg?height=671&width=643&top_left_y=1507&top_left_x=1171)
Fig. 2.7

Astigmatism may cause eye strain and also may be combined with nearsightedness or farsightedness.
Astigmatism can start in childhood or in adulthood.
Remedy: Cylindrical lens is use to correct astigmatism.
If the figure shown was constructed with all the radial lines of equal sharpness and contrast a person without astigmatism would see all these radial lines as perfectly sharp and with the same contrast. The diagram shown has been fudged to illustrate how it might appear to a person with astigmatism.

## Cataract

A cataract is a clouding of the lens in the eye. With people age, cataracts grow progressively darker and more dense, preventing light from easily passing through the lens. This results in vision loss.
Patients with cataracts have difficulty seeing in poorly lit environments. Many people also experience increased sensitivity to light and glare, double vision (or "ghost images"), and fading colors (blue may appear green; white may appear dull beige, etc.). Most cataracts are highly treatable. Cataract surgery is one of the most common surgeries performed in the world with $95 \%$ of patients experiencing improved vision if there are no other eye conditions present. During surgery, the doctor removes the clouded lens, and, in most cases, replaces it with an artificial lens, called an intraocular lens (IOL). An IOL is a clear, plastic lens that requires no care and becomes a permanent part of your eye. Thick eyeglass lenses after surgery might not be needed because of the implanted lens.
Complicated cataracts cannot be extracted like normal cataracts because of potential complications such as other eye conditions, lack of vision in the other eye, or health concerns such as diabetes.

Does short-sightedness (myopia) or long-sightedness (hypermetropia) imply necessarily that the eye has partially lost its ability of accommodation? If not, what might cause these defects of vision?

## SOLUTION

A person with normal ability of accommodation may be myopic or hypermetropic due to defective eye structure. When the eye ball from front to back gets too elongated the "myopic" defects occur, similarly when the eye ball from front to back gets too shortened the "hypermetropic" defects occur.
When the eye ball has normal length but the eyelens loses partially its ability of accommodation, the defect is called "presbyopia" and is corrected in the same manner as hypermetropia.

## ILLUSTRATION : 2.1

A man who wears the corrective lenses of power +3 D , has to hold a newspaper at 25 cm away to see the print clearly. How far away would the newspaper have to be, if he took off the corrective lenses and still, wanted a clear vision?

## SOLUTION :

Here, $u=-25 \mathrm{~cm} ; P=+3 \mathrm{D} ; v=$ ?
The corrective lenses have the focal length $f=\frac{1}{P}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~m}=\frac{100}{3} \mathrm{~cm}$
And the lens formula gives $\frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{-25}=\frac{1}{100 / 3} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{v}=\frac{3}{100}-\frac{1}{25} \Rightarrow v=-100 \mathrm{~cm}$
which means that the corrective lenses used by man form the clear images at 100 cm of the objects placed at 25 cm , i.e, the near point of man is at 100 cm . Therefore, without wearing the corrective lenses the man must hold the newspaper at 100 cm for the clear vision.

## ILLUSTRATION : 2.2

What should be the focal length of corrective lenses required by a person for reading a book, if his near point is at $\mathbf{9 0 ~ c m}$ ?

## SOLUTION :

The book is kept at the least distance of distance vision $D=25 \mathrm{~cm}$, i.e., $u=-25 \mathrm{~cm}$ and corrective lenses used form the clear images at 90 cm , which is the near point of person, i.e., $v=-90 \mathrm{~cm}$. Therefore, according to the lens formula, we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{v}-\frac{1}{u}=\frac{1}{f} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{-90}-\frac{1}{-25}=\frac{1}{f} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{f}=\frac{-5+18}{450} \Rightarrow f=34.6 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}
$$
