$\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{P}}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{1}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}}$
$\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{P}}=\mathrm{R} / 2$
$\frac{\mathrm{H}_{1}}{\mathrm{H}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}^{2} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{S}} \mathrm{V}^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{S}}}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2 \times 2 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{1}{4}=1: 4$.
(iv) (c) The bulb with the highest wattage glows with maximum brightness. Brightness of bulb $B (100 \mathrm{~W})$ is maximum.
Correct order of brightness will be, Bulb of $100 \mathrm{~W}>$ Bulb of $60 \mathrm{~W}>$ Bulb of 40 W .
(v) (c) Given, resistors, $R_{1}=2 \Omega$ and $\mathrm{R}_{2}=4 \Omega$ Voltgage, $V=6 \mathrm{~V}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-204.jpg?height=243&width=326&top_left_y=386&top_left_x=529)

Equivalent Resistance,
$=R_{1}+R_{2}=2+4=6 \Omega$ [Series combination]
Current, $I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{6}{6}=1 \mathrm{~A}$.
Heat dissipated in $4 \Omega$ Resistor
$=I^{2} R t=1 \times 4 \times 5=20 \mathrm{~J}$
$[\because I=1 \mathrm{~A}, R=4 \Omega, t=5 \mathrm{sec}$.]

## Exercise 2 Vaster NCERT (Textbook \& Exemplar)

## Text - Book Questions

1. A combination of electrical devices connected by conducting wires is called an electric circuit.
2. Its S.I unit is ampere. If one coulomb of charge flows through a conductor in one second, then the amount of current flowing is said to be 1 ampere.
3. Charge on one electron $=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$
$\therefore \quad 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$ charge carried by 1 electron
$\therefore 1 \mathrm{C}$ charge carried by
$=\frac{1}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=6.25 \times 10^{18}$ electrons
4. Cell is the device that helps to maintain the potential difference across a conductor.
5. It means that 1 joule of work is done in carrying one coulomb charge between the two given points.
6. $W=6 \times 1=6$ joules
7. Resistance of a conductor depends upon
(i) nature of material (resistivity $\rho$ )
(ii) length of wire ( $\ell$ )
(iii) area of cross section of wire $(A)$
8. The current will flow through a thick wire easily because the resistance of the thick wire (having area of cross-section large as compared to thin wire) is less than thin wire.
9. At constant temp.

$$
\begin{array}{lcr}
V \propto I & V_{1}=V & I_{1}=I \\
\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}=\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}} & V_{2}=\frac{V}{2} & I_{2}=? \\
\frac{V \times 2}{V}=\frac{I}{I_{2}} & \Rightarrow & I_{2}=\frac{I}{2}
\end{array}
$$

Current becomes half of its former value.
10. Alloys have high resistivity in comparison to pure metals. Also alloys do not oxidise readily at high temperatures but pure metals do. Therefore, alloys are used for making coils of electric toasters and electric irons rather than a pure metal.
11. (a) Iron is a better conductor than mercury because the resistivity of iron ( $=10 \times 10^{-8} \Omega \mathrm{~m}$ ) is less than the resistivity of mercury ( $=94 \times 10^{-8} \Omega \mathrm{~m}$ ).
(b) Silver having least resistivity $\left(=1.6 \times 10^{-8} \Omega \mathrm{~m}\right)$ is the best conductor.
12.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-204.jpg?height=257&width=554&top_left_y=525&top_left_x=1096)
13. $V=i R$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& V_{\text {total }}=6 \mathrm{~V}, \quad R_{\text {total }}=5+8+12=25 \Omega \\
& \therefore \quad i=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{6}{25} \mathrm{~A}
\end{aligned}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-204.jpg?height=259&width=579&top_left_y=982&top_left_x=1151)

Reading in the voltmeter $=V=i R$
$=\frac{6 \mathrm{~A}}{25} \times 12 \Omega=2.88 \mathrm{~V}$
14. (i) $\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{10^{6}}=\frac{10^{6}+1}{10^{6}}=\frac{1000001}{1000000}$

$$
R=\frac{1000000}{1000001} \Omega
$$

(ii) $\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{10^{3}}+\frac{1}{10^{6}}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{R}=\frac{10^{6}+10^{3}+1}{10^{6}}=\frac{1001001}{1000000} \\
& R=\frac{1000000}{1001001}=0.9 \Omega \text { (approx) }
\end{aligned}
$$

15. $\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{100}+\frac{1}{50}+\frac{1}{500}=\frac{5+10+1}{500}=\frac{16}{500}$

$$
R=\frac{500}{16}=31.25 \Omega
$$

$I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{220}{31.25}=7.04 \mathrm{~A}$
16. The equivalent resistance of resistors connected in parallel is less than even the smallest resistance connected in parallel, so, large current is obtained in the circuit for the same potential difference. So resistances are connecting in parallel.
17. (a)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-204.jpg?height=204&width=569&top_left_y=2366&top_left_x=1165)
(b)
$3 \Omega$ and $6 \Omega$ are connected in parallel and this combination is connected in series to $2 \Omega$ resistor to get an equivalent resistance of $4 \Omega$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-205.jpg?height=208&width=529&top_left_y=434&top_left_x=365)

All these three resistances are connected in parallel to get an equivalent resistance of $1 \Omega$.
18. (a) For highest resistance, resistors are connected in series.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{S} & =R_{1}+R_{2}+R_{3}+R_{4}=4+8+12+24 \\
& =48 \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

(b) For lowest resistance, resistors are connected in parallel. $\frac{1}{R_{p}}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{24}$

$$
=\frac{6+3+2+1}{24}=\frac{12}{24}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow R_{p}=2 \Omega
$$

19. According to the Joule's law of heating effect
$H=I^{2} R T \quad$ and $\quad H \propto R$
Because the resistance of heating element is very high. So more heat is developed, hence it glows. But the cord has very low resistance, so it does not glow.
20. $W=Q V$
$\Rightarrow H=Q V=96000 \times 50 \mathrm{~V}$
$H=4800000 \mathrm{~J}=4.8 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~J}$
$H=4.8 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~kJ}$
21. $H=I^{2} R t=5^{2} \times 20 \times 30=15000 \mathrm{~J}, H=15 \mathrm{~kJ}$
22. The rate at which energy is delivered by a current is called the power which is given by
$P=V I$
Thus, the potential difference ( $V$ ) determine the power delivered by a current.
23. $I=5 \mathrm{~A} ; V=220 \mathrm{~V}$
$P=V I=220 \times 5=1100 \mathrm{~W}$
Energy consumed $=P \times t=1100 \mathrm{~W} \times 2 h=2200 \mathrm{~Wh} =2.2 \mathrm{kWh}$.

## Text-Book Exercise

1. (d) Now, resistance of each wire is $R / 5$.

Equivalent resistance is given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=\frac{5}{R}+\frac{5}{R}+\frac{5}{R}+\frac{5}{R}+\frac{5}{R}=\frac{25}{R} \\
& \frac{1}{R^{\prime}}=25
\end{aligned}
$$

2. (b)
3. (d) $P=\frac{V^{2}}{R} \Rightarrow R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{220 \times 220}{100}=484 \Omega$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{220 \times 220}{100}=484 \Omega \\
& P=\frac{V^{2}}{R}=\frac{110 \times 110}{484}=25 \mathrm{~W}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. (c) $R_{S}=R_{1}+R_{2}=R+R=2 R$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{R_{P}}=\frac{1}{R_{1}}+\frac{1}{R_{2}}=\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R}=\frac{2}{R} \\
& R_{P}=R / 2 \\
& \frac{H_{1}}{H_{2}}=\frac{V^{2} R_{P}}{R_{S} V^{2}}=\frac{R_{P}}{R_{S}}=\frac{R}{2 \times 2 R}=\frac{1}{4}=1: 4 .
\end{aligned}
$$

5. Voltmeter is connected in parallel to the appliance across which the potential difference is to be measured.
6. $d=0.5 \mathrm{~mm}=0.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$
$\rho=1.6 \times 10^{-8} \Omega \mathrm{~m}$
$R=10 \Omega, \quad \ell=$ ?
$R=\rho \frac{\ell}{A} \Rightarrow \ell=\frac{R A}{\rho}$
$\therefore \quad \ell=\frac{10 \times 22 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{7 \times 4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-8}}=122.65 \mathrm{~m}$
If diameter is doubled, then resistance becomes one fourth.
7. Resistance from graph $=\frac{V_{2}-V_{1}}{I_{2}-I_{1}}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-205.jpg?height=333&width=460&top_left_y=1260&top_left_x=1283)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& R_{1}=\frac{3.4-1.6}{1-0.5}=\frac{1.8}{0.5}=3.6 \Omega \\
& R_{2}=\frac{6.7-3.4}{2.0-1.0}=3.3 \Omega \\
& R_{3}=\frac{10.2-6.7}{3-2}=3.5 \Omega ; \\
& R_{4}=\frac{13.2-10.2}{4-3}=3.0 \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Mean resistance,
$R=\frac{R_{1}+R_{2}+R_{3}+R_{4}}{4}=\frac{3.6+3.3+3.5+3}{4}$
$R=\frac{13.4}{4}$
$R=3.35 \Omega$
8. $V=12 \mathrm{~V} ; I=2.5 \mathrm{~mA}=2.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}$
$R=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{12 \mathrm{~V}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}=4.8 \times 10^{3} \Omega$
9. $R_{S}=0.2+0.3+0.4+0.5+12 \Omega=13.4 \Omega$
$I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{9 V}{13.4 \Omega}=0.67 A$
10. $\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{176}+\frac{1}{176}+---n$ times $=\frac{n}{176}$
$R=\frac{176}{n}$ and $I=\frac{V}{R} \Rightarrow 5=\frac{220 n}{176} \Rightarrow n=4$
Number of resistors $=4$.
11. (i) For getting a resistance of $9 \Omega$, the two resistors should be connected in parallel and one in series with them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in parallel, $R_{p}$ is given by

$$
\frac{1}{R_{p}}=\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{2}{6} \Rightarrow R_{p}=3 \Omega
$$

Now, the equivalent resistance of $R_{p}$ and $6 \Omega$ in series is given by
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-206.jpg?height=250&width=622&top_left_y=964&top_left_x=253)

$$
R=R_{p}+6=3+6=9 \Omega
$$

(ii) To get a resistance of $4 \Omega$, two resistors should be connected in series and one is parallel to them.
The equivalent resistance of the two resistors in series is given by
$R_{p}=6 \Omega+6 \Omega=12 \Omega$
Now, the equivalent resistance of $R_{p}$ and $6 \Omega$ in parallel is given by
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-206.jpg?height=218&width=620&top_left_y=1576&top_left_x=255)
$\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{R_{p}}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1+2}{12}=\frac{3}{12}=\frac{1}{4}$
$R=4 \Omega$
12. Resistance of each bulb $=R$ (say)
$R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{220 \times 220}{10}=4840 \Omega$
Let $n$ such bulbs be connected in parallel.
Then $\frac{1}{R_{P}}=\frac{1}{4840}+\frac{1}{4840}+\ldots \ldots . . n$ time $=\frac{n}{4840}$
$R_{p}=\frac{4840}{n} \Omega$
$R_{p}=\frac{V}{I} \quad \Rightarrow I=\frac{V}{R_{p}}$
$5 \leq \frac{220 n}{4840}$
$n \quad \leq 110$
Number of electric bulbs $=110$.
13. Case (i) $I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{220}{24}=9.2 \mathrm{~A}$

Case (ii) $R=24+24=48 \Omega$

$$
I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{220}{48}=4.6 A
$$

Case (iii) $\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{24}+\frac{1}{24}=\frac{2}{24}=\frac{1}{12}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& R=12 \Omega \\
\therefore \quad & I=\frac{220}{12}=18.3 \mathrm{~A} .
\end{aligned}
$$

14. Case (i)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-206.jpg?height=238&width=451&top_left_y=781&top_left_x=1235)

$$
R=1+2=3 \Omega \text { and } I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{6}{3}=2 \mathrm{~A}
$$

$\therefore \quad$ Voltage across $2 \Omega$ resistor $=I \times 2=2 \times 2=4 \mathrm{~V}$
$P_{1}=V I=4 \times 2=8 \mathrm{~W}$
Case (ii) $\quad I_{2}=\frac{V}{R_{2}}=\frac{4}{2}=2 \mathrm{~A}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-206.jpg?height=360&width=547&top_left_y=1320&top_left_x=1169)
$P_{2}$ across $2 \Omega$ resistor $=4 \times 2=8 \mathrm{~W}$
∴ ratio, $P_{1}: P_{2}=8: 8=1: 1$.
15. Resistance of first lamp $=R_{1}$

Resistance of second lamp $=R_{2}$
$R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}, \quad R_{1}=\frac{220 \times 220}{100}=484 \Omega$
$R_{2}=\frac{220 \times 220}{60}=\frac{2420}{3} \Omega$
They are connected in parallel
$\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{R_{1}}+\frac{1}{R_{2}}=\frac{1}{484}+\frac{3}{2420}$
$R=\frac{484 \times 2420}{3\left(484+\frac{2420}{3}\right)}=\frac{605}{2} \Omega$
$\therefore \quad I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{220 \times 2}{605}=\frac{8}{11} \mathrm{~A}$
$I=0.727 \mathrm{~A}$
16. $E=P \times t$

For T.V.
$E_{1}=250 \times 1 \times 3600$
For toaster
$E_{1}=900000 \mathrm{~J}$
$E_{2}=1200 \times 10 \times 60$
$E_{1}=9 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J}$
$E_{2}=720000 \mathrm{~J}$
$E_{2}=7.2 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~J}$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad E_{1}>\mathrm{E}_{2}
$$

$\therefore \quad$ T.V. set uses more energy.
17. $P=\frac{E}{t}=\frac{I^{2} R t}{t}=I^{2} R=15 \times 15 \times 8=1800 \mathrm{~W}$.

So, heat is developed at a rate of $1800 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{sec}$.
18. (a) Tungsten has very high melting point $\left(3380^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. So it can't melt upto a high temperature. This is the reason why tungsten is used almost exclusively for filament of electric lamps.
(b) Resistivity of an alloy is generally higher than that of its constituent metals.
(c) In series arrangement, the potential drop is divided into different appliances. So, some appliances can not get sufficient potential drop for its proper working. This is possible only in parallel connection. Hence, series arrangement is not used for domestic circuits.
(d) The resistance of a wire is inversely proportional to its area of cross-section, i.e., $R \propto \frac{1}{A}$.
(e) Copper and aluminium have low resistivity. Therefore, there will be less energy loss. Hence, they are usually empolyed for electricity transmission.

## Exemplar Questions

1. If a current larger than a specified value flows in a circuit, temperature of fuse wire increases to its melting point. The fuse wire melts and the circuit breaks.
2. Use the formula $R=\rho \frac{l}{A}$. Also, $V=R I$.
$R$ is doubled while V remains unchanged. Hence current becomes $\frac{I}{2}$
3. Provide the same potential difference across each electrical appliance.
4. (i) No. The resistance of the bulbs in series will be three times the resistance of single bulb. Therefore, the current in the series combination will be onethird compared to current in each bulb in parallel combination. The parallel combination bulbs will glow more brightly.
(ii) In series circuit bulbs will not glow because the circuit has broken.
5. (i) $R=R_{1} R_{2} /\left(R_{1}+R_{2}\right)=\left(\frac{8 \times 8}{8+8}\right)=4 \Omega$
(ii) $I=V / R_{\text {net }}$
$R_{\text {ent }}=4+4=8 \pi$
$I=\frac{8}{8} 1 \mathrm{~A}$
(iii) $V=I R=1 \times 4=4 \mathrm{~V}$
(iv) $P=I^{2} R=1^{2} \times 4=4 \mathrm{~W}$
(v) No difference.

Same current flows through each element in a series circuit.

55. (a) Same metal means same specific resistance

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{R_{1}}{R_{2}}=\frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow R_{2}=3 R_{1}=3 \times 10=30 \Omega \\
& R=R_{1}+R_{2}=40 \Omega
\end{aligned}

58. (d) $R=\frac{\rho \ell}{A} ; \ell=\frac{R A}{\rho}=\frac{4.2 \times \pi\left(\frac{0.4}{2} \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{4.8 \times 10^{-8}}=1.1 \mathrm{~m}$
59. (c) Ideal voltmeter should not draw any current flow source hence its resistance $=\infty$.
Practically infinite resistance is not possible, but ideal voltmeter is possible with the help of potentiometer that you will learn in higher classes.
60. (d) $R=\frac{\rho \ell}{A}=\frac{d \rho \ell^{2}}{m} ; R \propto \frac{\ell^{2}}{m}$

$$
\left[V=A \ell, d=\frac{m}{V}=\frac{m}{A \ell} \Rightarrow A=\frac{m}{d \ell}\right]
$$

$R_{1}: R_{2}: R_{3} \equiv \frac{9}{1}: \frac{4}{2}: \frac{1}{3} \equiv 9: 2: \frac{1}{3}=27: 6: 1$
61. (c) Same material → same density, specific resistance as they are material property.
$R=\frac{\rho \ell}{A}=\frac{\rho V}{A^{2}}=\frac{\rho m}{d A^{2}}$
$R \propto \frac{1}{A^{2}} \propto \frac{1}{r^{4}}$
$\frac{R_{A}}{R_{B}}=\frac{r_{B}^{4}}{r_{A}^{4}}=2^{4}=16 \Rightarrow R_{B}=\frac{24}{16}=1.5 \Omega$
62. (b) $R=\frac{\rho \ell}{A}=\frac{\rho \ell}{\pi r^{2}}$
$R^{\prime}=\frac{\rho 2 \ell}{\pi(2 r)^{2}}=\frac{R}{4}$
Specific resistance will remain same as it is a material property but remember it depends on temperature.
63. (a) Two resistance $(R / 2)$ will be in parallel, hence
$R_{e q}=R / 4$
64. (a) $V=i \times R ; R=60 / 15=4 \Omega$
65. (b)

69. (d) $2 \Omega, 4 \Omega, 2 \Omega$ on right side are in series resultant parallel to $8 \Omega$ then in series with $2 \Omega, 2 \Omega$ then in parallel with $8 \Omega$, then in series with $3 \Omega, 2 \Omega$. Thus, $R_{e q}=9 \mathrm{ohm}$.
$i=9 / 9=1$ amp flow from battery.
Passing through $3 \Omega$ it will divide into equal parts ( $1 / 2 \mathrm{amp}$ ) in $8 \Omega$ (near to cell) and remaining section then again divide into equal parts ( $1 / 4 \mathrm{amp}$ ) in $8 \Omega$ (middle one) and remaining section hence $1 / 4$ amp. passes through $4 \Omega$.

80. (d) Fuse wire should be such that it melts immediatley when strong current flows through the circuit. The same is possible if its melting point is low and resistivity is high.
81. (a) A heating wire should be such that it produces more heat when current is passed through it and also does not melt. It will be so if it has high specific resistance and high melting point.

Potential over $8 \Omega=R i_{2}=8 \times \frac{1}{2}=4 \mathrm{~V}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-208.jpg?height=195&width=197&top_left_y=2101&top_left_x=1190)

86. (d) In house electrical circuits the fuse wire for safety should be of high resistance and low melting point.
87. (b) An the three resistors are connected in parallel
$\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e} q}}=\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R}+\frac{1}{R} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{R_{e q}}=\frac{3}{R} R_{e q}=\frac{R}{3}$
88. (d) Resistance $(R)=\frac{\rho L}{A}$

Length is stretched to double
$L^{\prime}=2 L$
Area $A^{\prime}=\frac{A}{2} \quad \therefore R^{\prime}=\frac{\rho \times 2 L}{\frac{A}{2}}$
$R^{\prime}=4 \frac{\rho L}{A} \Rightarrow R^{\prime}=4 R$
$R=1 \Omega \quad \therefore$ New Resistance, $R^{\prime}=4 \Omega$
89. (a) Here, metallic conductor can be considered as the combination of various conductors connected in series. And in series combination current remains same.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-208.jpg?height=238&width=373&top_left_y=1366&top_left_x=1256)
90. (d) $\because 10 \Omega$ and $20 \Omega$ are in series $=(10+20) \Omega=30 \Omega$
and $10 \Omega$ and $5 \Omega$ are in series $=(10+5) \Omega=15 \Omega$
$R_{\text {eff }}=\frac{30 \times 15}{15+30}=\frac{450}{45}=10 \Omega$
So the total current $I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{30}{10}=3$ Ampere
In branch CA current $=1 \mathrm{~A}$
In branch CB current $=2 \mathrm{~A}$
$\therefore V_{C}-V_{A}=10$ Volt
$\& V_{C}-V_{B}=20$ Volt

Subtracting (i) from (ii), $V_{A}-V_{B}=10$ volt.
92. (d) Let the Diameter of wire $=\frac{d}{5}$

Radius will be $=\frac{r}{5}$
91. (a)

Changed Area will be $=\mathrm{A}=\pi \mathrm{r}^{2}$
$=\pi\left(\frac{r}{5}\right)^{2}=\frac{\pi r^{2}}{25} \Rightarrow A=\frac{\pi r^{2}}{25} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 25 A=\pi r^{2}$
Hence stretched length will be $=25 l$
Change resistance $(R)=\frac{\rho \ell}{A}=\frac{\rho(25 \ell)}{A / 25}=625 R$
93. (a) Resistance of the heater be R .

New resistance of heater is $\mathrm{R} / 2$
Initial power $=\frac{V^{2}}{R} \quad$ Final power $=\frac{V^{2}}{R / 2}=2 \frac{V^{2}}{R}$
$\therefore \quad$ Heat generated is doubled.
94. (d) $R=\frac{\rho \ell}{A}$; New area $=n A \therefore$ New length $=\frac{\ell}{n}$
$\Rightarrow \quad R^{\prime}=\frac{\rho \ell}{n^{2} A}=\frac{R}{n^{2}}$
95. (b) The potential difference in each loop is zero.
∴ No current will flow or current in each resistance is Zero.
96. (b) We know that, $R=\frac{\rho \ell}{A}$
or $\quad R=\frac{\rho \ell^{2}}{\text { Volume }} \Rightarrow R \propto \ell^{2}$
According to question $\ell_{2}=\mathrm{n} \ell_{1}$
$\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}=\frac{n^{2} l_{1}^{2}}{l_{1}^{2}}$
or, $\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}=n^{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad R_{2}=n^{2} R_{1}$
97. (b) Resistance of $40 \mathrm{~W}-200 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~W}-200 \mathrm{~V}$,
$100 \mathrm{~W}-200 \mathrm{~V}$ are respectively.
$R_{40}=\frac{V^{2}}{P_{40}}=\frac{200 \times 200}{40}=1000 \Omega$
$R_{50}=800 \Omega$ and $\mathrm{R}_{100}=400 \Omega$
$I=\frac{600}{1000+800+400}=\frac{600}{2200}=0.2727 \mathrm{~A}$
$I_{40}=\frac{P_{1}}{V}=\frac{40}{200}=0.2 \mathrm{~A}$
$I_{50}=\frac{P_{2}}{V}=\frac{50}{200}=\frac{5}{20}=0.25 \mathrm{~A}$
$I_{100}=\frac{P_{3}}{V}=\frac{100}{200}=0.5 \mathrm{~A}$
Clearly, $0.2 \mathrm{~A} \& 0.25 \mathrm{~A}<0.27 \mathrm{~A}$ hence both 40 W and 50 W bulbs will fuse.
98. (b) When current flows through a conductor of tapered cross-section, current flow through every section remains constant i.e., $I_{1}=I_{2}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-209.jpg?height=200&width=497&top_left_y=310&top_left_x=1260)
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{j_{1}}{j_{2}}=\frac{A_{2}}{A_{1}}<1$
or, $\quad j_{1}<j_{2}$
i.e., Current density ( $j$ ) increases in the narrow region.
Also, $j=\operatorname{nev}_{d} \Rightarrow n e v_{d_{1}}<n e v_{d_{2}}$
or, $V_{d_{1}}<V_{d_{2}}$
i.e., Drift velocity ( $v_{d}$ ) increases in the narrow region.
And, Electric field, $E=j \rho$
where, $\rho=$ resistivity of material.
Clearly, $E_{1}<E_{2}$
99. (a) In series grouping equivalent resistance $R_{\text {series }}=\mathrm{nR}$

In parallel grouping equivalent resistance
$R_{\text {parallel }}=\frac{R}{n}$
$I=\frac{E}{n R+R}$
$10 I=\frac{E}{\frac{R}{n}+R}$

Dividing eq. (ii) by (i),
$10=\frac{(n+1) R}{\left(\frac{1}{n}+1\right) R}$
Solving we get, $n=10$
100. (b) After simplifying the given circuit, we get,
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-209.jpg?height=282&width=372&top_left_y=1790&top_left_x=1324)
$R_{A B}=\frac{1}{3} \Omega$
Then equivalent resistance across the battery,
$R_{e q}=2+\frac{1}{3}+2=\frac{13}{3} \Omega$
So current in circuit, $I=\frac{V}{R_{e q}} \Rightarrow \frac{1.3}{13} \times 3 \mathrm{amp}$

$$
I=\frac{3}{10} \mathrm{amp}
$$

Power dissipated across arm $A B$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& P_{A B}=I^{2} \times R_{A B}=\left(\frac{3}{10}\right)^{2} \times \frac{1}{3} \\
& P_{A B}=\frac{3}{100}=0.03 \text { Watt }
\end{aligned}
$$

Total power dissipated in circuit,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& P_{c k t}=I^{2} \times R_{e q}=\left(\frac{3}{10}\right)^{2} \times \frac{13}{3} \\
& P_{c k t}=\frac{39}{100}=0.39 \mathrm{watt}
\end{aligned}
$$

Ratio of power across $A$ and $B$ to total power $=$ Ratio of work done across $A$ and $B$ to total circuit $\because W=P \times t$

So, $\frac{P_{A B}}{P_{c k t}}=\frac{W_{A B}}{W_{c k t}}=\frac{0.03}{0.39}=\frac{1}{13}$
101. (b) After simplifying the given circuit, we get
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-210.jpg?height=440&width=560&top_left_y=1048&top_left_x=283)

Resistance between $\operatorname{arm} A B, R_{\text {net } A B}=\frac{1}{5} k \Omega=\frac{1000}{5} \Omega$
Resistance between arm $B C, R_{\text {net } B C}=\frac{1}{3} \Omega=\frac{1000}{3} \Omega$
So, $R_{\text {net }}=R_{\text {net } A B}+R_{\text {net } B C}$
We get, $R_{\text {net }}=\frac{1000}{5}+\frac{1000}{3}$
$R_{\text {net }}=\frac{8000}{15} \Omega$
According to ohm's law, $V=I R$
$I=\frac{8 \times 15}{8000}=15 m A$
102. (b) Here $R_{D A}=R_{A B}=R_{B C}=R / 4$
and $R_{D E}=R_{E C}=R / 8$

$\therefore \quad R_{e q}=\frac{\left(\frac{7 R}{8}\right)\left(\frac{R}{8}\right)}{R}=\frac{7 R}{64}$

105. (c) Ammeter : In series connection, the same current flows through all the components. It aims at measuring the current flowing through the circuit and hence, it is connected in series.
Voltmeter : A voltmeter measures voltage change between two points in a circuit. So we have to place the voltmeter in parallel with the cicuit component.

Let $R_{1}+R_{2}$ be the resistances of the two heaters and $H$ be the heat produced.

## Physics

$\therefore \quad H=\frac{V^{2}}{R_{1}} t_{1}=\frac{V^{2}}{R_{2}} t_{2}$
When used in series, $H=\frac{V^{2} t}{R_{1}+R_{2}}$
When used in parallel, $H=\left(\frac{V^{2}}{R_{1}}+\frac{V^{2}}{R_{2}}\right) t$

## Assertion \& Reason

1. (c) It is clear that in a battery circuit, the point of lowest potential is the negative terminal of battery. and current flows from higher potential to lower potential.
2. (c) It is common error to say that $\mathrm{V}=\mathrm{Ri}$ is a statement of Ohm's law. The essence of Ohm's law is that the value of $R$ is independent of the value of $V$. The equation $V=R i$ is used for finding resistance of all conducting devices, whether they obey Ohm's law or not.
3. (d) (A) will read zero but (V) will read E
4. (d) $\rho$ is the characteristic of the material of resistors. It does not depend on the length and cross-sectional area of resistors. But R depends on the length and the cross-sectional are of the resistor.
So, $R_{1}$ may be greater than $R_{2}$ even when $\rho_{1} \leq \rho_{2}$.
5. (d) Conductivity $=\frac{1}{\text { Resistivity }}$
$\therefore \quad$ Conductivity × resistivity $=1$
6. (a) 7. (d)
7. (c) Resistivity is a material property.
8. (c) $\rho=\rho_{0}(1+\alpha \Delta T)$
9. (b) Glow $=\operatorname{Power}(P)=I^{2} R$
$\therefore \quad \frac{d P}{P}=2\left(\frac{d I}{I}\right)=2 \times 0.5=1 \%$
10. (a) Power loss $=i^{2} R=\left(\frac{P}{V}\right)^{2} R$
[ $P=$ Transmitted power]
11. (b) $P=\frac{V^{2}}{R} ; \quad R \propto \frac{1}{P}$ (same rated voltage)
12. (a) 14. (c)

$$

5. (19.2) $\mathrm{P}=3000 \mathrm{~W}, \mathrm{~V}=240 \mathrm{~V}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& P=V I \Rightarrow I=\frac{P}{V}=\frac{3000}{240}=12.5 \mathrm{~A} \\
& V=I R \Rightarrow R=\frac{V}{I}=\frac{240}{12.5}=19.2 \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

6. (1.41)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-212.jpg?height=289&width=492&top_left_y=998&top_left_x=1215)
$R_{1}=7 \Omega, R_{2}=2 \Omega, R_{3}=10 \Omega$,
$R_{4}=30 \Omega, R_{5}=10 \Omega, \mathrm{~V}=6 \mathrm{~V}$
Now, $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$ are in series,
$R^{\prime}=R_{1}+R_{2}=7+2=9 \Omega$
Also, $R_{4}$ and $R_{5}$ are in series,
$R^{\prime \prime}=R_{4}+R_{5}=30+10=40 \Omega$
Now, $R^{\prime}, R^{\prime \prime}$ and $R_{3}$ are in parallel,
$\frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{R^{\prime}}+\frac{1}{R^{\prime \prime}}+\frac{1}{R_{3}}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{R}=\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{40}+\frac{1}{10}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{R}=\frac{40+9+36}{360}=\frac{85}{360}$
$\therefore \quad R=\frac{360}{85}=4.23 \Omega$
$V=I R \Rightarrow I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{6}{4.23}=1.41 \mathrm{~A}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-218.jpg?height=426&width=481&top_left_y=324&top_left_x=1379)

## (CONCEPTMAP

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-218.jpg?height=288&width=171&top_left_y=2323&top_left_x=251)

Properties of Magnets

- Attractive property
- Magnet attracts magnetic materials e.g. iron, cobalt, etc.
- Directive property
- When a magnet is suspended freely, it always be in N-S direction
- Opposite poles attract and like poles repel.
- Poles exists in pairs
- Repulsion is a sure test of magnet

$$
\begin{array}{|c|}
\hline \begin{array}{c}
\star \text { On the axis of } \\
\text { circular loop }
\end{array} \\
\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{NIa}^{2}}{2\left(\mathrm{r}^{2}+\mathrm{a}^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}} \\
\text { If } \mathrm{a} \gg \mathrm{r} \\
B=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{0}}{4 \mathrm{p}}>\frac{2 N i A}{a^{3}} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

## Magnetic Effects of Electric Current

An electric current flowing in a conductor produces magnetism.

Magnetic Field

Space around a magnet in which magnetic effect is experienced
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-218.jpg?height=246&width=368&top_left_y=2086&top_left_x=805)

Magnetic Field Due to A Current Carrying Conductor

Biot-Savart's law Magnetic field due to current carrying element,

$$
\mathrm{dB}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{I d l \sin \theta_{\hat{n}}}{r^{2}}
$$

★Magnetic field due to a current carrying circular loop
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-218.jpg?height=131&width=681&top_left_y=1665&top_left_x=505)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-218.jpg?height=259&width=400&top_left_y=1529&top_left_x=1178)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-218.jpg?height=543&width=335&top_left_y=1784&top_left_x=1190)

★ Horizontal component of earth's magnetic field Total intensity can be resolved into vertical component $\left(\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{v}}\right)$ and horizontal component $\left(\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{H}}\right) . \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{v}}=\mathrm{B} \sin \theta, \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{H}}=\mathrm{B} \cos \theta$

ElectroMagnet A soft metal core made into magnet by the passage of electric current through a coil surrounding it.
*Force acting on a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{F} & =\mathrm{qVB} \sin \theta \\
& =\mathrm{q}(\mathrm{~V} \times \mathrm{B})
\end{aligned}
$$

particle, if field
Zero force i.e.,
$\mathrm{F}=0$ on a charged
particle, if field $\vec{B}=0$ charge $\mathrm{q}=0$ charge is at rest, $\mathrm{v}=0$
when $\theta=0^{\circ}$ or $180^{\circ}$
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-218.jpg?height=450&width=66&top_left_y=1074&top_left_x=1170)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-218.jpg?height=391&width=251&top_left_y=1135&top_left_x=1249)
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-218.jpg?height=394&width=167&top_left_y=1132&top_left_x=1510)

$$
F=k \frac{m_{1} m_{2}}{r^{2}}
$$

> ElectroMagnetic Induction Voltage is induced by the relative motion between a wire and a magnetic field.

> Electric Generator It converts mechanical energy to electrical energy works on the principle of electromagnetic induction.

${ }^{\star}$ Magnetic field due to a bar magnet
*At end on position

$$
\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 \mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{~d}^{3}}
$$

${ }^{\star}$ At broadside on position

$$
\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0} \quad \mathrm{M}}{4 \pi\left(\mathrm{r}^{2}+\ell^{2}\right) 3 / 2}
$$

$\stackrel{\star}{\text { Torque experienced }} \tau=\mathrm{MB} \sin \theta$

## OERSTED EXPERIMENT

Magnetic effect of electric current means-an electric current flowing in a conductor produces a magnetic field in the space around it. In 1820, Hans Christian Oersted Showed that electricity and magnetism are related phenomena.

Oersted discovered a magnetic field around a conductor carrying electric current.
(a) A magnet at rest produces a magnetic field around it while an electric charge at rest produces an electric field around it.
(b) A current carrying conductor has a magnetic field and not an electric field around it. On the other hand, a charge moving with a uniform velocity has an electric as well as a magnetic field around it.
(c) An electric field cannot be produced without a charge whereas a magnetic field can be produced without a magnet.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-219.jpg?height=478&width=550&top_left_y=699&top_left_x=762)
(d) No poles are produced in a coil carrying current but such a coil shows north and south polarities.
(e) All oscillating or an accelerated charge produces E.M. waves also in addition to electric and magnetic fields.

## ARTIFICIAL MAGNETS

Magnets produced from magnetic materials are called artificial magnets. They can be made in a variety of shapes and sizes and are used extensively in electrical apparatus.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-219.jpg?height=296&width=1433&top_left_y=1475&top_left_x=390)
Fig. 4.2

Artificial magnets are generally made from special iron or steel alloys which are usually magnetized electrically. The material to be magnetized is inserted into a coil of insulated wire and a heavy flow of electrons is passed through the wire. Magnets can also be produced by stroking a magnetic material with magnetite or with another artificial magnet. The forces causing magnetization are represented by magnetic lines of force.
Artificial magnets are usually classified as permanent or temporary, depending on their ability to retain their magnetic properties after the magnetizing force has been removed. Magnets made from substances, such as hardened steel and certain alloys which retain a great deal of their magnetism, are called permanent magnets. These materials are relatively difficult to magnetize because of the opposition offered to the magnetic lines of force as the lines of force try to distribute themselves throughout the material. The opposition that a material offers to the magnetic lines of force is called reluctance. All permanent magnets are produced from materials having a high reluctance.
A material with a low reluctance, such as soft iron or annealed silicon steel, is relatively easy to magnetize but will retain only a small part of its magnetism once the magnetizing force is removed. Materials of this type that easily lose most of their magnetic strength are called temporary magnets. The amount of magnetism which remains in a temporary magnet is referred to as its residual magnetism. The ability of a material to retain an amount of residual magnetism is called the retentivity of the material.
Magnets are also described in terms of the permeability of their materials, or the ease with which magnetic lines of force distribute themselves throughout the material. A permanent magnet, which is produced from a material with a high reluctance, has a low permeability. A temporary magnet, produced from a material with a low reluctance, would have a high permeability.

## PROPERTIES OF MAGNETS

(1) Attractive property and poles : When a magnet is dipped into iron fillings it is found that the concentration of iron filings, i.e., attracting power of the magnet is maximum at two points near the ends and minimum at the centre. The places in a magnet where its attracting power is maximum are called poles while the place of minimum attracting power is called the neutral region.
(2) Directive property and N-S poles : When magnet is suspended its length becomes parallel to N-S direction. The pole pointing north is called the north $(\mathrm{N})$ pole while the other pointing south is called the south $(\mathrm{S})$ pole.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-220.jpg?height=282&width=1074&top_left_y=625&top_left_x=466)
Fig. 4.3

(3) Opposite poles ( N and S ) attract and like poles ( N and N , or S and S ) repel one another.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-220.jpg?height=170&width=1194&top_left_y=1009&top_left_x=431)
Fig. 4.4

The line joining the two poles of a magnet is called magnetic axis and the vertical plane passing through the axis of a freely suspended or pivoted magnet is called magnetic meridian.
Magnetic Length ( $\mathbf{2} \ell$ ) : The distance between two poles along the axis of a magnet is called its effective or magnetic length.
As poles are not exactly at the ends, the effective length is lesser than the actual length of the magnet.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-220.jpg?height=316&width=1090&top_left_y=1398&top_left_x=459)
Fig. 4.5

(4) Poles exist in pairs : In a magnet the two poles are found to be equal in strength and opposite in nature. If a magnet is broken into number of pieces, each piece becomes a magnet with two equal and opposite poles. This shows that monopoles do not exist.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-220.jpg?height=154&width=979&top_left_y=1905&top_left_x=513)
Fig. 4.6

(5) Repulsion is a sure test of polarity : A pole of a magnet attracts the opposite pole while repels similar pole. A sure test of polarity is repulsion and not attraction, as attraction can take place between opposite poles or a pole and a piece of unmagnetised magnetic material due to 'induction effect'.
(6) Magnetic induction : A magnet attracts certain other substances through the phenomenon of magnetic induction i.e., by inducing opposite pole in a magnetic material on the side facing it as shown in fig.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-220.jpg?height=184&width=624&top_left_y=2295&top_left_x=724)
Fig. 4.7

Seeds of two tomatoes varieties Rocco and Monza were treated by passing them through an artificial magnetic field (MF) with a constant defined velocity before seeding. The seedlings obtained from MF treated and non MF-treated seeds were planted into the MF treated and non MF-treated plots. They were irrigated by MF-treated and non MF treated water.

Observations were made on early-yield, total-yield, beginning of blooming, and quality of fruit. While significant differences were not observed in Rocco, important MF effects were clearly seen on Monza. Yield increases on Monza in magnet treated plots were around $\mathbf{2 8} \boldsymbol{-} \mathbf{5 1}$ percent, especially in early yields, and Monza bloomed three-four days earlier..

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## Pole Strength and Magnetic Moment of a Magnet

The strength of attracting a magnetic material by a magnetic pole is measured by pole strength, generally denoted by $m$. The north and south poles of a magnet are said to be associated with the pole strengths $+m$ and $-m$, respectively, each of which is a scalar, having the dimensional formula [ $\mathrm{M}^{\circ} \mathrm{LT}^{\circ} \mathrm{A}$ ] and SI unit Am.
Each pole strength is imagined to be distributed over the entire cross-section of the magnet. Therefore, if the magnet is cut along it's length into the two equal parts, each part will have it's poles of the strengths $+m / 2$ and $-m / 2$, respectively, and if the magnet is cut perpendicular to it's length into the two equal parts, each part will have it's poles of same initial strengths $+m$ and $-m$, respectively.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-221.jpg?height=302&width=1024&top_left_y=1320&top_left_x=651)
Fig. 4.8

The magnetic moment of a magnet is defined as a vector $\vec{M}$ whose magnitude is $M=m(2 l)$
where $m$ is the pole strength of magnet and $2 l$ is the effective length of magnet and whose direction is from the south pole to the north pole. It has the dimensional formula [ $\mathrm{M}^{\circ} \mathrm{L}^{2} \mathrm{~T}^{\circ} \mathrm{A}$ ] and $\mathbf{S I}$ unit $\mathrm{Am}^{2}$.
If the magnet is cut along it's length, or perpendicular to its length, into the two equal parts, each part will have it's magnetic moment of magnitude one-half of that of the original magnetic moment, as shown above.

## Coulomb's Law in Magnetism

This law defines the force $F$ between the two magnetic poles of the strengths $m_{1}, m_{2}$ at a distance $r$ apart in a medium as
$F=\left(\frac{m_{1} m_{2}}{r^{2}}\right)$
In S.I. units $K=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}=10^{-7} \mathrm{~Wb} A-1 m^{-1}$ and
$k=\mu_{0}=1$ (unitless) in CGS. Evidently, the force $F$ is directly proportional to the product of the pole strengths and inversely proportional to the square of the distance $r$. It acts along the line joining the two magnetic poles. It is attractive for the unlike magnetic poles and repulsive for the like magnetic poles. It depends on the medium in which the magnetic poles are situated.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-221.jpg?height=218&width=408&top_left_y=2288&top_left_x=1509)
Fig. 4.9

CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING TOPIC CONNECTING

Time to Check Your Knowledge
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-222.jpg?height=159&width=177&top_left_y=310&top_left_x=388)

A friend tells you that a refrigerator door, beneath its layer of white-painted plastic, is made of aluminum. How could you check to see if this is true (without any scraping) ?

## SOLUTION

I will bring a magnet and make it touch with the refrigerator door. If the magnet shows a feeble attraction with it then I can say that it is aluminum.

## ILLUSTRATION : 4.1

Four identical bar magnets, each of magnetic moment $M$, are arranged to form a square, as shown. Calculate the resultant magnetic moment of the system.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-222.jpg?height=277&width=305&top_left_y=909&top_left_x=877)
Fig. 4.10

## SOLUTION :

The magnetic moments of the given four identical bar magnets are acting along the sides of a square, as shown. The resultant of each of the two pairs of magnetic moments, i.e., $(1,4)$ and $(2,3)$, is $M \sqrt{2}$ and it is represented by the same diagonal of the square. Therefore, the resultant of all the four magnetic moments, taken together, is $2 \sqrt{2} M$, which is acting along the diagonal.

## MAGNETIC FIELD AND MAGNETIC FIELD LINES

## Magnetic Field

The space around a magnet (or a current carrying conductor) in which its magnetic effect can be experienced is called the magnetic field. It is a quantity that has both direction and magnitude.
The magnetic field in a region is said to be uniform if the magnitude of its strength and direction is same at all points in that region.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-222.jpg?height=287&width=1271&top_left_y=1781&top_left_x=367)
Fig. 4.11

The strength of magnetic field is also known as magnetic induction or magnetic flux density.
The SI unit of strength of magnetic field is tesla (T)
1 tesla $=1$ newton ampere ${ }^{-1}$ metre $^{-1}\left(\mathrm{NA}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}\right)=1$ weber metre ${ }^{-2}\left(\mathrm{~Wb} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}\right)$
The cgs unit is gauss (G), 1 gauss (G) $=10^{-4}$ tesla (T).
Magnetic Field Lines
A line such that the tangent at any point on it gives the direction of the magnetic field at that point is called a magnetic field line or magnetic lines of force.

## Properties of Magnetic Field Lines

(i) All field lines are closed curves. They come out of the magnet from the side of the north pole and go into it on the side of the south pole. They continue inside the magnet.
(ii) The field lines are close together near the poles and spread out away from them.
(iii) Two field lines never intersect each other.

## Physics

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-223.jpg?height=156&width=446&top_left_y=308&top_left_x=226)

## Mapping of Magnetic Lines of Force

- Fix a sheet of white paper on a drawing board using some adhesive material. - Place a bar magnet in the centre of it.
- Sprinkle some iron filings uniformly around the bar magnet. A salt-sprinkler may be used for this purpose.
- Now tap the board gently. The iron filings rearrange themselves in the form of curves.

The curves represent the magnetic lines of force.

## MAGNETIC FIELD DUE TO MAGNETIC POLE

In SI, the magnetic induction $\vec{B}$ due to a magnetic pole at a point at a distance $r$ is defined as the force per unit north pole placed there, having the magnitude

$$
B=\left(\frac{\mu}{4 \pi}\right)\left(\frac{m}{r^{2}}\right) \text { tesla } \Rightarrow \quad H=\frac{B}{\mu}=\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi}\right)\left(\frac{m}{r^{2}}\right) \mathrm{A} / \mathrm{m}
$$

And direction away for north pole and towards for the south pole.
In CGS, the magnetic field intensity $\vec{H}$ due to a magnetic pole at a point at a distance $r$ is defined as the force per unit north pole placed there, having the magnitude

$$
H=\left(\frac{1}{\mu}\right)\left(\frac{m}{r^{2}}\right) \text { oersted } \Rightarrow B=\mu H=\left(\frac{m}{r^{2}}\right) \text { gauss }
$$

And direction away for the north pole and towards for the south pole.

## FORCE ON MAGNETIC POLE PLACED IN MAGNETIC FIELD

In SI, the force on a magnetic pole of strength $m$ placed in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is $\vec{F}= \pm m \vec{H}$ where + sign is for the north pole and - sign is for the south pole. Thus, the force is in the direction of $\vec{B}$ for the north pole and in the opposite direction for the south pole

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-223.jpg?height=122&width=448&top_left_y=1539&top_left_x=852)
Fig. 4.12

In CGS, the force on a magnetic pole of strength $m$ placed in a magnetic field $\vec{H}$ is $\vec{F}= \pm m \vec{H}$ where + sign is for the north pole and - sign is for the south pole. Thus, the force is in the direction of $\vec{H}$ for the north pole and in the opposite direction for the south pole.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-223.jpg?height=145&width=449&top_left_y=1809&top_left_x=847)
Fig. 4.13

## CONNECTING TOPIC

## BIOT-SAVART'S LAW

The Biot-Savart law defines the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ due to a current distribution. Let us consider a line distribution of steady current $I$ confined to a filamentary wire ST. The wire may be imagined to be subdivided into the many elementary parts, each characterised by a vector $d l$ whose magnitude is equal to the length of elementary part and whose direction is that of the current, as shown.
The magnetic field at a point $P$ due to an elementary part is

$$
d \vec{B}=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi}\right)\left(\frac{d \vec{l} \times \vec{R}}{R^{3}}\right)
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-223.jpg?height=525&width=346&top_left_y=2087&top_left_x=1539)
where $\vec{R}$ is the position vector of point $P$ with respect to the elementary part. This magnetic field has the magnitude

$$
d B=\left(\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi}\right)\left(\frac{d l \sin \theta}{R^{2}}\right) \text { this is Biot-savart's law }
$$

And the direction of the cross product $(d \vec{l} \times \vec{R})$, i.e., inwards and perpendicular to the plane of figure, as shown. The magnetic field $\vec{B}$ at the point $P$ due to the complete wire ST is obtained by performing a summation process over all the elementary parts. The direction of $\vec{B}$ will be the same as that of an elementary magnetic field $d \vec{B}$.

## Magnetic Field Due to a Current Carrying Conductor

From Oersted experiment followed by Ampere we can conclude that a magnetic field is developed around a conductor when electric current is passed through it. This observation is called magnetic effect of electric current. The presence of a current in a wire near a magnetic compass affect the direction of the compass needle. We now know that current gives rise to magnetic fields, just as electric charge give rise to electric fields.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-224.jpg?height=414&width=524&top_left_y=726&top_left_x=1334)
Fig. 4.15: Compass near a current-carrying wire

## Magnetic Field due to a Current Through a Straight Conductor

The magnetic field around a conductor carrying current is in the form of closed circular loops, in a plane perpendicular to the conductor, and is given by right hand thumb rule.

## Right Hand Thumb Rule

If we grasp the conductor in the palm of the right hand so that the thumb points in the direction of the flow of current, then the direction in which the fingers curl, gives the direction of magnetic field lines.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-224.jpg?height=486&width=784&top_left_y=1509&top_left_x=612)
Fig. 4.16

For the current flowing through the conductor in the direction shown in figures (a) or (b), the rule predict that magnetic field lines will be in anticlockwise direction, when seen from above.
This rule is also called Maxwell's corkscrew rule. If we consider ourselves driving a corkscrew in the direction of the current, then the direction of the corkscrew is the direction of the magnetic field.

## Ampere's Swimming Rule

Imagine a man swimming along the wire, in the direction of current, (such that the current enters at his feet and leaves him at his head) and facing towards a compass needle placed underneath the wire, then the magnetic field produced is such that the north pole of the compass needle gets deflected towards his left hand.

## Magnetic field due to long straight conductor

$\mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi r}\left(\sin \theta_{1}+\sin \theta_{2}\right) \quad \frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi}=10^{-7} \frac{\text { tesla }-m}{\text { amp } .}$
If the conductor is infinitely long, then $\theta_{1}=90^{\circ}$ and $\theta_{2}=90^{\circ}$
$B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi r}\left[\sin \frac{\pi}{2}+\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right]=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi r}[1+1]=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 I}{r}$
or $B=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi r}$
If conductor is of infinite length but one end is in front of point P i.e. one end of conductor starts from point N then
$\theta_{1}=0^{\circ}$ and $\theta_{2}=90^{\circ} \therefore \mathrm{B}=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi r}$
Conductor is finite length and point P is just in front of middle of the conductor

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-225.jpg?height=588&width=448&top_left_y=431&top_left_x=1446)
Fig. 4.17

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi r}(2 \sin \theta) & {\left[\theta_{1}=\theta_{2}=\theta\right]} \\
B=\frac{\mu_{0} \mathrm{I} L}{2 \pi r \sqrt{L^{2}+4 r^{2}}} & \sin \theta=\frac{\mathrm{L} / 2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{\mathrm{~L}}{2}\right)^{2}+\mathrm{r}^{2}}}
\end{array}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-225.jpg?height=311&width=373&top_left_y=1062&top_left_x=1521)
Fig. 4.18

## ILLUSTRATIOM : 4.2

## Compute the magnetic field at a point of 9 cm from the long straight wire carrying a current of $\mathbf{6} \mathbf{A}$.

## SOLUTION :

Here, $a=9 \mathrm{~cm}=9 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}, I=6 A, B=? B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi a}=\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 6}{2 \pi \times 9 \times 10^{-2}}=1.33 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$

## Magnetic Field Due to a Current Through a Circular Loop

At every point of a current-carrying circular loop, the concentric circles representing the magnetic field around it would become larger and larger as we move away from the wire. By the time we reach at the centre of the circular loop, the arcs of these big circles would appear as straight lines. Every point on the wire carrying current would give rise to the magnetic field appearing as straight lines at the center of the loop. By applying the right hand rule, it is easy to check that every section of the wire contributes to the magnetic field lines in the same direction within the loop.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-225.jpg?height=444&width=588&top_left_y=1699&top_left_x=1334)
Fig. 4.19 (a)

## Magnetic Field due to a Current in a Solenoid

A coil of many circular turns of insulated copper wire wrapped closely in the shape of a cylinder is called a solenoid. The pattern of the magnetic field lines around a current carrying solenoid is shown in Fig. 4.19(b). One end of the solenoid behaves as a magnetic north pole, while the other behaves as the south pole. The field lines inside the solenoid are in the form of parallel straight lines. This indicates that the magnetic field is the same at all points inside the solenoid. That is, the field is uniform inside the solenoid A strong magnetic field produced inside a solenoid can be used to magnetise a piece of magnetic material, like soft iron, when placed inside the coil

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-225.jpg?height=352&width=440&top_left_y=2193&top_left_x=1484)
Fig. 4.19 (b)

## CASE STUDY-1 : Understand Magnetic Field \& its Strength and Field Lines

| A moving charge or a current of charges produces a magnetic field. If the charged particle moves in a magnetic field, the magnetic character of a charge in motion becomes evident. The charged particle experiences a deflecting force. The force is greatest when the particle moves in a direction perpendicular to the magnetic field lines. At other angles, the force is less, and it becomes zero when the particle moves parallel to the field lines. Force on a current carrying conductor in a magnetic field, $\vec{F}=I \vec{l} \times \vec{B}$ magnetic field $\vec{B}$ due to an infinitely long straight wire carrying a current i, at a distance ' $r$ ' $\vec{B}=\frac{\mu_{0} i}{2 \pi r}$ |
| :--- |
| CASE-I : Why is iron magnetic or attracted by a magnet or an electromagnet but wood not? Wood have no magnetic domains that can be induced into alignment. But the domains in the unmagnetised piece of iron are induced into alignment by the magnetic field of the magnet.![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-226.jpg?height=317&width=232&top_left_y=1191&top_left_x=682) |
| CASE - II : The magnetic field at a mid point in between the two in finitely long parallel wires carry equal current in same direction is zero. At mid-point, magnetic fields due to both the wires are equal and opposite. <br> ∴ Net magnetic field, $\boldsymbol{B}_{\text {Net }}=\mathbf{0}$![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-226.jpg?height=151&width=180&top_left_y=1191&top_left_x=928) |

## 응 Think Out of the Box

CASE - III : A wire carrying current I and other carrying $2 I$ in the same direction produces a magnetic field ' $B$ ' at the mid-point. Thene is no change in the field when 2I wire is switched off.

When both conductors crrying current then net magnetic field at the mid point $B=\frac{\mu_{0} 2 I}{2 \pi r}-\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}=\frac{\mu I}{2 \pi r}$

When current $2 I$ is switched off,then magnetic field due to conductor carrying current $I, B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 \pi r}$

CASE - IV : The electron remains stationary even a strong magnetic field is applied on a stationary electron. Magnetic force acts on a moving charge not on a stationary electron or charge.

CASE - V : Magnetic field strength inside a current carrying loop of wire is greater than the field strength about a straight section of current carrying wire?

If the wire is bent into a loop, the magnetic field lines become bunched up inside the loop. hence magnetic field strength inside a current carrying loop of wire is greater than the field strength about a straight section of a current carrying wire.
Q. 1 What would happen to harmful cosmic radiations i.e., harmful charged particles from outer space on to the earth, if there is no earth's magnetic field? Ans: If there is no earth's magnetic field, then the intensity of harmful cosmic radiations striking the earth's surface increases. Because of earth's magnetic field harmful cosmic radiations are deflected.

## Magnetic field at the centre of a circular current-carrying coil

$$
B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{2 r} \quad \text { For a coil of } n \text { turns } B=\frac{\mu_{0} n I}{2 r}
$$

Magnetic Field due to part of current carrying circular conductor (Arc)

$$
B=\frac{\mu_{0} I}{4 \pi r} \alpha
$$

Magnetic field on the axis of a circular coil

$$
B=\frac{\mu_{0} N \mathrm{I} a^{2}}{2\left(a^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-227.jpg?height=264&width=232&top_left_y=593&top_left_x=1448)
Fig.4.20

At the centre of the loop, $x=0$
$B=\frac{\mu_{0} N \mathrm{I} a^{2}}{2 a^{3}}$
or $B=\frac{\mu_{0} N \mathrm{I}}{2 a}$

In terms of area $\mathrm{A}\left(=\pi a^{2}\right)$ of the circular current loop, the quantity N IA is known as the magnetic dipole moment M of the current loop.
Magnetic field at point well inside the solenoid is $\mathbf{B}_{\mathbf{i n}}=\mu_{\mathbf{0}} \mathbf{n i}$
Wher $n=\frac{N}{l}=$ number of turns per unit length

## Magnetic dipole moment of the current loop

The current loop can be regarded as a magnetic dipole which produces its magnetic field and magnetic dipole moment of the current loop is equal to the product of ampere turns and area of current loop, we can write

$$
\begin{aligned}
& B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{N \mathrm{I} A}{a^{3}} \text { or } B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 \mathrm{NI} \mathrm{~A}}{\mathrm{a}^{3}} \\
& \therefore B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M}{a^{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the observation point is far away from the coil, then $a \ll x$.
So, $a^{2}$ can be neglected in comparison to $x^{2}$.
$\therefore B \frac{\mu_{0} N \mathrm{I} a^{2}}{2 x^{3}}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-227.jpg?height=396&width=451&top_left_y=1379&top_left_x=1411)
Fig. 4.21

In terms of magnetic dipole moment, $B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 M}{x^{3}} \quad\left[B=\frac{\mu_{0}}{2 \pi} \frac{N \mathrm{I} A}{x^{3}}=\frac{\mu_{0}}{4 \pi} \frac{2 N \mathrm{I} A}{x^{3}}\right]$

## RIGHT HAND PALM RULE

If we hold the thumb of right hand mutually perpendicular to the grip of the fingers such that the curvature of the finger represents the direction of current in the wire loop, then the thumb of the right hand will point in the direction of magnetic field near the centre of the current loop.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-227.jpg?height=358&width=1650&top_left_y=2142&top_left_x=205)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-228.jpg?height=291&width=531&top_left_y=443&top_left_x=742)
