Using Einstein's equation $h v=W_{0}+\frac{1}{2} m v_{\text {max }}^{2}$

$$
5.47 \mathrm{eV}=W_{0}+1.3 \mathrm{eV} \Rightarrow W_{0}=4.17 \mathrm{eV}
$$

Red light of wavelength $6328 \AA$ will have energy of each photon

$$
E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{6328 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=1.96 \mathrm{eV}\left(\because 1=227 \mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{o}} \text { given }\right)
$$

Thus energy of red light photons is less than work function 4.17 eV , hence irrespective of any intensity no emission will take place.
8. The total energy of an electron in the first excited state of the hydrogen atom is about $\mathbf{- 3 . 4} \mathbf{e V}$.
(a) What is the kinetic energy of the electron in this state?
(b) What is the potential energy of the electron in this state?
(c) Which of the answer above would change if the choice of the zero of potential energy is changed?

Sol. Kinetic energy of an electron in an orbit, $\mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{e(Z e)}{2 r}$

Potential energy of an electron in an orbit $U=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{Z e^{2}}{r}\right)$

Total energy $E=K+U=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}\left(\frac{Z e^{2}}{2 r}\right)=-K=\frac{U}{2}$
It is given, total energy $E=(-3.4 \mathrm{eV})$
(a) Kinetic energy of electron in this state $E=-K$

So, $K=E=-(-3.4 \mathrm{eV})=3.4 \mathrm{eV}$
(b) Potential energy $E=U / 2$

$$
U=2 E=2(-3.4)=-6.8 \mathrm{eV}
$$

(c) If the zero of the potential energy is chosen differently, the kinetic energy remain the same. Althugh potential energy and hence total energy changes.
9. A radioactive isotope has a half-life of $\boldsymbol{T}$ years. How long will it take the activity to reduce to (a) $\mathbf{3 . 1 2 5 \%}$, (b) $\mathbf{1 \%}$ of its original value?
Sol. Activity $R=R_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$
Also instantaneous activity, $R=-\lambda N \quad R=-\frac{0.693}{T} N$
Initital activity, $R_{0}=-\lambda N_{0} \quad$ So, $R_{0}=-\frac{0.693}{T} N_{0}$
(a) $\frac{R}{R_{0}}=\frac{N}{N_{0}}=\frac{3.125}{100}=\frac{1}{32} \quad$ or $\quad \frac{N}{N_{0}}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{5} \quad$ or $\quad n=5 \quad \therefore \quad t=n T=5 T$ years.
(b) $\frac{R}{R_{0}}=\frac{N}{N_{0}}=\frac{1}{100}$

Required time, as cannot be solved by direct calculation as in part (a).
$t=\frac{2.303}{\lambda} \log \frac{N_{0}}{N}=\frac{2.303 T}{0.693} \log 100=\frac{2.303 \times 2 \times T}{0.693} \approx 6.65 T$ years.
10. The half-life of ${ }_{38}^{90} \mathrm{Sr}$ is 28 years. What is the distintegration rate of 15 mg of this isotope?

Sol. Given: $T_{1 / 2}=28$ years $=28 \times 3.154 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~s}$
Mass $m=15 \mathrm{mg}=0.015 \mathrm{~g}$
Number of atoms in 0.015 g sample of ${ }_{38}^{80} \mathrm{Sr}$,

$$
N=\frac{m}{M} \times \text { Avogadro's number }=\frac{0.015 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms }}{90}
$$

Activity of the sample, $R=\lambda / N=\frac{0.693}{T_{1 / 2}} N=\frac{0.693 \times 0.015 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}}{28 \times 3.154 \times 10^{7} \times 90}$
$=7.877 \times 10^{10}$ disintegration $/ \mathrm{sec}=7.877 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~Bq}=\frac{7.877 \times 10^{10}}{3.7 \times 10^{10}} \mathrm{Ci}=2.13 \mathrm{Ci}$.
11. The diode used in the circuit shown has constant voltage drop at 0.5 V at all currents and a maximum power rating of 100 milli-watts. What should be the value of the resistor $R$, connected in series with diode and obtaining maximum current?

Sol. Given : $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{P}}=0.5 \mathrm{~V}$, maximum power rating $\mathrm{P}=100 \mathrm{~mW}=100 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~W}$ and source voltage $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{s}}=1.5 \mathrm{~V}$.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-404.jpg?height=314&width=435&top_left_y=1991&top_left_x=785)

Now diode resistance $R_{D}=\frac{V_{p}^{2}}{P}=\frac{(0.5)^{2}}{100 \times 10^{-3}}=2.5 \Omega$
Current in diode $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{D}}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{D}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{D}}}=\frac{0.5}{2.5}=0.2 \mathrm{~A}$
sગ!ડᲩ૫d
∴ Total resistance in the circuit $R_{T}=\frac{V_{s}}{\mathrm{I}_{D}}=\frac{1.5}{0.2}=7.5 \Omega$
So, resistance R in circuit $=R_{T}-R_{D}=7.5-2.5=5 \Omega$
12. The circuit shown in fig. contains two diodes each with a forward resistance of $50 \Omega$ and with infinite reverse resistance. If the battery voltage is 6 V , find the current through the $100 \Omega$ resistance.
Sol. In this circuit, diode $\mathrm{D}_{1}$ is forward biased and offers a resistance of $50 \Omega$. The diode $\mathrm{D}_{2}$ is reverse biased and offers infinite resistance to the current i.e. no current flows through $D_{2}$. Thus the equivalent circuit is as shown in fig.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/f9bdfe7f-53d6-4e8d-8a01-8a4d11db1458-405.jpg?height=373&width=1195&top_left_y=731&top_left_x=476)

Total resistance of circuit $=50+150+100=300 \Omega$
Current through $100 \Omega, \mathrm{I}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{6}{300}=0.02 \mathrm{~A}$.

(b) the helium nucleus has more momentum than the thorium nucleus.
(c) the helium nucleus has less kinetic energy than the thorium nucleus.
(d) the helium nucleus has more kinetic energy than the thorium nucleus.
36. The radius of a nucleus is given by $r_{0} A^{1 / 3}$, where $r_{0}=1.3 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{~m}$ and $A$ is the mass number of the nucleus. The lead nucleus has $A=206$. The electrostatic force between two protons in this nucleus is approximately
[KVPY]
(a) $10^{2} \mathrm{~N}$
(b) $10^{7} \mathrm{~N}$
(c) $10^{12} \mathrm{~N}$
(d) $10^{17} \mathrm{~N}$
37. Half-lives of two radioactive elements A and B are 20 minutes and 40 minutes, respectively. Initially, the samples have equal number of nuclei. After 80 minutes, the ratio of decayed number of A and B nuclei will be :
[NTSE]
(a) $1: 4$
(b) $5: 4$
(c) $1: 16$
(d) $4: 1$
38. When a metallic surface is illuminated with radiation of wavelength $\lambda$, the stopping potential is V . If the same surface is illuminated with radiation of wavelength $2 \lambda$, the stopping potential is $\frac{\mathrm{V}}{4}$. The threshold wavelength for the metallic surface is :
[JSTSE]
(a) $4 \lambda$
(b) $5 \lambda$
(c) $\frac{5}{2} \lambda$
(d) $3 \lambda$
39. Given the value of Rydberg constant is $10^{7} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$, the wave number of the last line of the Balmer series in hydrogen spectrum will be :
[JSTSE]
(a) $0.025 \times 10^{4} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$
(b) $0.5 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$
(c) $0.25 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$
(d) $2.5 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}$
40. Particles used in the Rutherford's scattering experiment to deduce the structure of atoms
[KVPY]
(a) had atomic number 2 and were fully ionised
(b) had atomic number 2 and were neutral
(c) had atomic number 4 and were fully ionised
(d) had atomic number 4 and were neutral
41. A radioactive nucleus A with a half life T , decays into a nucleus B . At $\mathrm{t}=0$, there is no nucleus B . At sometime t , the ratio of the number of B to that of A is 0.3 . Then, t is given by
[NTSE]
(a) $t=T \log (1.3)$
(b) $t=\frac{T}{\log (1.3)}$
(c) $t=T \frac{\log 2}{\log 1.3}$
(d) $t=\frac{T \log 1.3}{\log 2}$
42. The photoelectric threshold wavelength of silver is 3250 $\times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$. The velocity of the electron ejected from a silver surface by ultraviolet light of wavelength $2536 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$ is
(Given $\mathrm{h}=4.14 \times 10^{-15} \mathrm{eVs}$ and $\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ )
[JSTSE]
(a) $\approx 0.6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
(b) $\approx 61 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
(c) $\approx 0.3 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
(d) $\approx 6 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
43. The ratio of wavelengths of the last line of Balmer series and the last line of Lyman series is :-
[JSTSE]
(a) 1
(b) 4
(c) 0.5
(d) 2
44. Radioactive material 'A' has decay constant ' $8 \lambda$ ' and material ' $B$ ' has decay constant ' $\lambda$ '. Initially they have same number of nuclei. After what time, the ratio of number of nuclei of material ' B ' to that ' A ' will be $\frac{1}{e}$ ?
[JSTSE]
(a) $\frac{1}{7 \lambda}$
(b) $\frac{1}{8 \lambda}$
(c) $\frac{1}{9 \lambda}$
(d) $\frac{1}{\lambda}$
45. If the series limit frequency of the Lyman series is $\mathrm{v}_{1}$, then the series limit frequency of the P -fund series is :
[NTSE]
(a) $25 v_{\mathrm{L}}$
(b) $16 v_{\mathrm{L}}$
(c) $v_{\mathrm{L}} / 16$
(d) $v_{\mathrm{L}} / 25$
46. The ratio of kinetic energy to the total energy of an electron in a Bohr orbit of the hydrogen atom, is
[JSTSE]
(a) $1: 1$
(b) $1:-1$
(c) $1:-2$
(d) $2:-1$
47. The nuclear radius is given by $R=r_{0} A^{1 / 3}$, where $r_{0}$ is constant and $A$ is the atomic mass number.
[KVPY] Then,
(a) The nuclear mass density of $\mathrm{U}^{238}$ is twice that of $\mathrm{Sn}^{119}$
(b) The nuclear mass density of $\mathrm{U}^{238}$ is thrice that of $\mathrm{Sn}^{119}$
(c) The nuclear mass density of $\mathrm{U}^{238}$ is the same as that of $\mathrm{Sn}^{119}$
(d) The nuclear mass density of $\mathrm{U}^{238}$ is half that of $\mathrm{Sn}^{119}$
48. Taking the wavelength of first Balmer line in hydrogen spectrum ( $n=3$ to $n=2$ ) as 660 nm , the wavelength of the $2^{\text {nd }}$ Balmer line ( $\mathrm{n}=4$ to $\mathrm{n}=2$ ) will be;
[NTSE]
(a) 889.2 nm
(b) $\quad 488.9 \mathrm{~nm}$
(c) 642.7 nm
(d) $\quad 388.9 \mathrm{~nm}$
49. An electron is accelerated through a potential difference of $10,000 \mathrm{~V}$. Its de Broglie wavelength is, (nearly): ( $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{e}}=9 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$ )
[JSTSE]
(a) $12.2 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~m}$
(b) $12.2 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~m}$
(c) $12.2 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~m}$
(d) 12.2 nm
50. $\alpha$-particle consists of:
[JSTSE]
(a) 2 protons and 2 neutrons only
(b) 2 electrons, 2 protons and 2 neutrons
(c) 2 electrons and 4 protons only
(d) 2 protons only
51. The total energy of an electron in an atom in an orbit is -3.4 eV . Its kinetic and potential energies are, respectively:
[JSTSE]
(a) $-3.4 \mathrm{eV},-3.4 \mathrm{eV}$
(b) $-3.4 \mathrm{eV},-6.8 \mathrm{eV}$
(c) $3.4 \mathrm{eV},-6.8 \mathrm{eV}$
(d) $3.4 \mathrm{eV}, 3.4 \mathrm{eV}$
52. A particle is moving 5 times as fast as an electron. The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelength of the particle to that of the electron is $1.878 \times 10^{-4}$. The mass of the particle is close to :
[NTSE]
(a) $4.8 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~kg}$
(b) $9.1 \times 10^{-31} \mathrm{~kg}$
(c) $1.2 \times 10^{-28} \mathrm{~kg}$
(d) $9.7 \times 10^{-28} \mathrm{~kg}$
53. Light of frequency 1.5 times the threshold frequency is incident on a photosensitive material. What will be the photoelectric current if the frequency is halved and intensity is doubled?
[JSTSE]
(a) four times
(b) one-fourth
(c) zero
(d) doubled

## One or More Than One Option Correct

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 54-71) : This section contains multiple choice questions. Each question has 4 choices (a), (b), (c) and (d) out of which one or more may be correct.
54. Which of the following nuclei will not produced due to one $\alpha$-decay of the nucleus ${ }_{Z} X^{\mathrm{A}}$ is/are
(a) ${ }_{\mathrm{Z}+2} \mathrm{Y}^{\mathrm{A}+4}$
(b) ${ }_{\mathrm{Z}} \mathrm{Y}^{\mathrm{A}}$
(c) ${ }_{\mathrm{Z}-2} \mathrm{Y}^{\mathrm{A}-4}$
(d) ${ }_{\mathrm{Z}-4} \mathrm{Y}^{\mathrm{A}-2}$
55. According to Bohr's theory of hydrogen atom, for the electron in the $n$th allowed orbit, the
(a) linear momentum is proportional to $(1 / n)$
(b) radius is proportional to $n$
(c) the kinetic energy is proportional to $\left(1 / n^{2}\right)$
(d) the angular momentum is proportional to $n$
56. An electron orbiting in a circular orbit around the nucleus of an atom
(a) has a magnetic dipole moment
(b) exerts an electric force on the nucleus equal to that on it by the nucleus
(c) does produces a magnetic induction at the nucleus
(d) has a net energy inversely proportional to its distance from the nucleus
57. A radioactive substance has an average life of 5 hours. In a time of 5 hours
(a) Half of the active nuclei decay
(b) Less than half of the active nuclei decay
(c) More than half of the active nuclei decay
(d) All active nuclei decay
58. In which of the following situations the heavier of the two particles has smaller de Broglie wavelength? The two particles
(a) move with the same speed
(b) move with the same linear momentum
(c) move with the same kinetic energy
(d) have fallen through the same height
59. In the hydrogen atom in the ground state
(a) the kinetic energy of the electron is less than the potential energy which is positive
(b) the potential energy is less than the kinetic energy which is positive
(c) the potential energy is negative and the kinetic energy numerically less than the numerical value of potential energy
(d) the total energy is negative
60. Mark the correct options
(a) An atom with a vacancy has a smaller than the neutral atom
(b) KX-ray is emitted when a hole makes a jump from the $K$-shell to some other shell
(c) The wavelength of K X -ray is smaller than the wavelength of $L \mathrm{X}$-ray of the same material
(d) The wavelength of $K_{\alpha} \mathrm{X}$-ray is smaller than the wave length of $K_{\beta}$ X-ray of the same material
61. Which of the following is/are subatomic particles?
(a) Protons
(b) Positron
(c) Neutrino
(d) Photon
62. When the nucleus of an electrically neutral atom undergoes a radioactive decay process, it will remain neutral after the decay if the process is
(a) an $\alpha$-decay
(b) a $\beta$-decay
(c) a $\gamma$-decay
(d) a $K$-capture process
63. When a nucleus with atomic number $Z$ and mass number $A$ undergoes a radioactive decay process
(a) both $Z$ and $A$ will decrease if the process is $\alpha$-decay
(b) $Z$ will decrease but $A$ will not change if the process is $\beta^{+}$- decay
(c) $Z$ will increase but A will not change, if the process is $\beta^{-}$- decay
(d) $Z$ and $A$ will remain unchanged if the process is $\gamma$ - decay
64. During a $\beta^{-}$- decay which of the following statements are correct?
(a) The daughter nucleus has one proton less than the parent nucleus
(b) The daughter nucleus has one proton more than the parent nucleus
(c) An electron which is already present within the nucleus is rejected
(d) A neutron in the nucleus decays emitting an electron
65. If ${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{238}$ undergoes successively $8 \alpha$-decays and 6 $\beta$-decays, then resulting nucleus is/are
(a) ${ }_{82} \mathrm{U}^{206}$
(b) ${ }_{82} \mathrm{~Pb}^{206}$
(c) ${ }_{82} \mathrm{U}^{210}$
(d) ${ }_{82} \mathrm{U}^{214}$
66. A sample of radioactive element has a mass of 10 g at an instant $t=0$. The approximate mass of this element in the sample after two mean lives is/are
(a) 2.50 g
(b) 3.70 g
(c) 6.30 g
(d) 1.35 g
67. Two radioactive samples have decay constant $15 x$ and $3 x$. If they have the same number of nuclei initially, the ratio of number of nuclei after a time $\frac{1}{6 x}$ is/are
(a) $e^{-2}$
(b) $\frac{e}{2}$
(c) $\frac{1}{e^{2}}$
(d) $\frac{2 e}{3}$
68. The heavier nuclei tend to have larger $N / Z$ ratio because
(a) a neutron is heavier than a proton
(b) a neutron is an unstable particle
(c) a neutron does not exert electric repulsion
(d) Coulomb force has longer range as compared to the nuclear force
69. ${ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{235}$ is an alpha active nucleus. Then in a large quantity of the element
(a) the probability of a nucleus disintegrating during one second is lower in the first half-life and greater in the fifth half-life
(b) the probability of a nucleus disintegrating during one second remains constant for all the time
(c) quite an appreciable quantity of $\mathrm{U}^{235}$ will remain, even after average life
(d) the energy of emitted $\alpha$-particle is less than the disintegration energy of the $\mathrm{U}^{235}$ nucleus
70. During the radioactive decay
(a) atomic mass number cannot increase
(b) atomic number may increase
(c) atomic number may decrease
(d) atomic number may remain unchanged
71. An electron in a hydrogen atom makes a transition $n_{1} \rightarrow n_{2}$ where $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$ are principle quantum numbers of the two state. Assume the Bohr model to be valid. The time period of the election in the initial state is eight times that in the final state. The possible values of $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$ are
(a) $n_{1}=4, n_{2}=2$
(b) $n_{1}=8, n_{2}=2$
(c) $n_{1}=8, n_{2}=1$
(d) $n_{1}=6, n_{2}=3$

## Passage Based Questions

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 72-77) : Study the given paragraph(s) and answer the following questions.

## Passage - I

A physicist wishes to eject electrons by shining light on a metal surface. The light source emits light of wavelength of 450 nm . The table lists the only available metals and their work functions.

| Metal | $\mathbf{W}_{\mathbf{0}} \mathbf{( e V )}$ |
| :--- | :---: |
| Barium | 2.5 |
| Lithium | 2.3 |
| Tantalum | 4.2 |
| Tungsten | 4.5 |

72. Which metal(s) can be used to produce electrons by the photoelectric effect from given source of light?
(a) Barium only
(b) Barium or lithium
(c) Lithium, tantalum or tungsten
(d) Tungsten or tantalum
73. Which option correctly identifies the metal that will produce the most energetic electrons and their energies?
(a) Lithium, 0.45 eV
(b) Tungston, 1.75 eV
(c) Lithium, 2.30 eV
(d) Tungston, 2.75 eV
74. Suppose photoelectric experiment is done separately with these metals with light of wavelength 450 nm . The maximum magnitude of stopping potential amongst all the metals is-
(a) 2.75 volt
(b) 4.5 volt
(c) 0.45 volt
(d) 0.25 volt

## Passage - II

In a radioactive decay chain, ${ }^{232}{ }_{90}$ Th nucleus decays to ${ }^{212}{ }_{82} \mathrm{~Pb}$ nucleus. Let $\mathrm{N}_{\alpha}$ and $\mathrm{N}_{\beta}$ be the number of $\alpha$ and $\beta^{-}$particles, respectively, emitted in this decay process.
75. Number of $\alpha$-particles $\left(\mathrm{N}_{\alpha}\right)$ decay in the process is
(a) 2
(b) 4
(c) 5
(d) 6
76. Number of $\beta^{-}$-particles $\left(\mathrm{N}_{\beta}\right)$ decay in the process is
(a) 6
(b) 2
(c) 4
(d) 5
77. Change in atomic number during the process is
(a) 8
(b) 7
(c) 10
(d) 9

## Assertion \& Reason

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 78-83) : Each of these questions contains an Assertion followed by Reason. Read them carefully and answer the question on the basis of following options. You have to select the one that best describes the two statements.
(a) If both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
(b) If both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion.
(c) If Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
(d) If Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.
78. Assertion : Nuclear forces are independent of charges.

Reason : Nuclear force is not a central force.
79. Assertion : Radioactive nuclei emit $\beta^{-}$particles.

Reason : Electrons exist inside the nucleus.
80. Assertion : Binding energy (or mass defect) of hydrogen nucleus is zero.
Reason : Hydrogen nucleus contain only one nucleon.
81. Assertion : The rest mass energy of a nucleus is smaller than the rest mass energy of its constituent nucleons in free state.
Reason : Nucleons are bound together in a nucleus.
82. Assertion : In a decay process of a nucleus, the mass of products is less than that of the parent.
Reason : The rest mass energy of the products must be less than that of the parent.
83. Assertion : In street light circuits, photo-cells are used to switch on and off the lights automatically at dusk and dawn.
Reason : A photocell can convert a change in intensity of illumination into a change in photocurrent that can be used to control lighting system.

## Multiple Matching Questions

DIRECTIONS(Qs. 84): Following question has four statements ( $A, B, C$ and $D$ ) given in Column $I$ and 5 statements ( $p, q, r, s$ and t) in Column II. Any given statement in Column I can have correct matching with one or more statement(s) given in Column II. Match the entries in column I with entries in column II.
84. Match the following

|  |  |  |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| A. Column-I |  |  |  |
| B. One $\alpha$-particle decay |  |  |  |
| C. | ${ }_{14} \mathrm{Si}^{31}$, | ${ }_{15} \mathrm{P}^{32}$ | (q) |
| D. | Moderator |  | (s) |
|  | A | B | C |
| (a) | q | r | p |
| (b) | s | p | q |
| (c) | $\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{q}$ | s | $\mathrm{r}, \mathrm{s}$ |
| (d) | $\mathrm{q}, \mathrm{s}$ | $\mathrm{q}, \mathrm{r}$ | q |

## Column-II

(p) Isotones
(q) $X$-rays
(r) decrease in atomic number by two
(s) to slow down neutrons

## Integer/ Numeric Questions

DIRECTIONS (Qs. 85-90) : Following are integer based/ Numeric based questions. Each question, when worked out will result in one integer or numeric value.
85. The mean lives of a radio-active substances are 1620 years and 405 years for $\alpha$-emission and $\beta$-emission respectively. Find out the time during which three-fourth of a sample will decay if it is decaying both by $\alpha$-emission and $\beta$-emission simultaneously.
86. Find the binding energy per nucleon for a lithium nucleus ${ }_{3} \mathrm{Li}^{7}$. Take mass of ${ }_{3} \mathrm{Li}^{7}=7 \mathrm{u}$, mass of proton 1.007825 $u$ and mass of neutron $=1.008665 u$. Take $1 u=931.5$ MeV .
87. $x$-alpha and $y$-beta particles are emitted when uranium $\left({ }_{92} \mathrm{U}^{238}\right)$ decays to lead ${ }_{82} \mathrm{~Pb}^{206}$. The value of $x$ and $y$ are ?
88. A radioactive nucleus undergoes a series of decay according to the scheme

$$
\mathrm{A}^{\alpha} \mathrm{A}_{1} \xrightarrow{\beta} \mathrm{~A}_{2} \xrightarrow{\alpha} \mathrm{~A}_{3} \xrightarrow{\gamma} \mathrm{~A}_{4} \text {. }
$$

If the mass number and atomic number of A are 180 and 72 respectively, what are these numbers for $\mathrm{A}_{4}$ ?
89. Some amount of a radioactive substance (half life $=10$ days) is spread inside a room and consequently the level of radiation becomes 50 times permissible level for normal occupancy of the room. After how many days the room will be safe for occupation?
90. The work function of metal is 1 eV . Light of wavelength 3000 Angustrum is incident on this metal surface. Find the maximum velocity of the emitted photo-electrons.

## Exercise Foundation Builder +

| 1. | (c) | 2. | (c) | 3. | (d) | 4. | (d) | 5. | (c) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 6. | (d) | 7. | (c) | 8. | (c) | 9. | (d) | 10. | (d) |
| 11. | (c) | 12. | (c) | 13. | (c) | 14. | (a) | 15. | (b) |
| 16. | (c) | 17. | (d) | 18. | (b) | 19. | (b) | 20. | (c) |

24. (c) Substance left undecayed - $N_{0}-\frac{3}{4} N_{0}=\frac{1}{4} N_{0}$

$$
\frac{N}{N_{0}}=\frac{1}{4}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n}
$$

$\therefore \mathrm{n}=2$ i.e. in two half lives
$\therefore \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{nT}=2 \times 4=8$ months
25. (a) $r_{n}=r_{0} \cdot n^{2}$, where $r_{0}$ is radius of G-state \& $r_{n}$ is radius of $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ state. (For first excited state $\mathrm{n}=2$ ).
26. (a) Amount left $=\mathrm{N}_{0} / 2^{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{N}_{0} / 8$ (Here $\mathrm{n}=15 / 5=3$ )
27. (a) Because thermal energy decreases, therefore mass should increase.
28. (a)
29. (a) For forward biasing of $p-n$ junction, the positive terminal of external battery is to be connected to $p$-semiconductor and negative terminal of battery to the n -semiconductor.
30. (c) Semiconductors are insulators at room temperature.
31. (d) Due to heating, when a free electron is produced then simultaneously a hole is also produced.
32. (d) $\beta^{-}$-particles are electron which are emitted from following nuclear reaction:
${ }_{0}^{1} n \rightarrow{ }_{1}^{1} p+{ }_{-1}^{0} e+\bar{v}$
So, emitting nucleus changes into another nucleus of higher proton number.
${ }_{Z}^{A} X \rightarrow{ }_{z+1}^{4} Y+{ }_{-1}^{0} e+\bar{v}$
$\frac{N}{Z}<1$
Hence $Z>N$
33. (c) $\mathrm{U}=-\mathrm{K} \frac{\mathrm{ze}^{2}}{\mathrm{r}} ; \mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{E}=-\frac{\mathrm{k}}{2} \frac{\mathrm{ze}^{2}}{\mathrm{r}}$
$\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{k}}{2} \frac{\mathrm{ze}^{2}}{\mathrm{r}}$. Here r decreases
34. (b) Given : Work function $\phi$ of metal $=2.28 \mathrm{eV}$

Wavelength of light $\lambda=500 \mathrm{~nm}=500 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{KE}_{\max } & =\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi \\
\mathrm{KE}_{\max } & =\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{5 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}-2.28 \\
\mathrm{KE}_{\max } & =2.48-2.28=0.2 \mathrm{ev} \\
\lambda_{\min } & =\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}(\mathrm{KE})_{\max }}} \\
& =\frac{\frac{20}{3} \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 9 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}} \\
\lambda_{\min } & =\frac{25}{9} \times 160^{-9} \\
& =2.80 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

35. (d) In an explosion a body breaks up into two pieces of unequal masses both part will have numerically equal momentum and lighter part will have more velocity.
$\mathrm{U} \rightarrow \mathrm{Th}+\mathrm{He}$
$\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{Th}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{Th}}}, \mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{He}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}^{2}}{2 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{He}}}$
since $\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{He}}$ is less so $\mathrm{KE}_{\mathrm{He}}$ will be more.
36. (a) Force of electrostatic repulsion, between two protons
$F=\frac{k(e)(e)}{r^{2}}=\frac{k e^{2}}{\left(r_{0} A^{1 / 3}\right)^{2}}=\frac{k \cdot e^{2}}{r_{0}^{2} \cdot A^{2 / 3}}$
Substituting values in above equation,

$$
\begin{aligned}
F & =\frac{9 \times 10^{9} \times\left(16 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2}}{\left(1.3 \times 10^{-15}\right)^{2}(206)^{2 / 3}} \\
& =0.039 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{~N} \approx 10^{2} \mathrm{~N}
\end{aligned}
$$

37. (b) For $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{t} / 2}=20 \mathrm{~min}, \mathrm{t}=80 \mathrm{~min}$, number of half lifes $\mathrm{n}=4$
∴ Nuclei remaining $=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}}{2^{4}}$.
Therefore nuclei decayed $=\mathrm{N}_{0}-\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}}{2^{4}}$
For $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{t}^{1 / 2}}=40 \mathrm{~min} ., \mathrm{t}=80 \mathrm{~min}$, number of half lifes $\mathrm{n}=2$
∴ Nuclei remaining $=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}}{2^{2}}$.

Therefore nuclei decayed $=\mathrm{N}_{0}-\frac{\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{o}}}{2^{2}}$
∴ Required ratio $=\frac{\mathrm{No}-\frac{\mathrm{No}}{2^{4}}}{\mathrm{No}-\frac{\mathrm{No}}{2^{2}}}=\frac{1-\frac{1}{16}}{1-\frac{1}{4}}=\frac{15}{16} \times \frac{4}{3}=\frac{5}{4}$
38. (d) According to Einstein's photoelectric effect,
$\mathrm{eV}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}-\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}$
$\mathrm{eV} / 4=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{2 \lambda}-\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}$

Dividing equation (i) by (ii) by
$\Rightarrow \quad 4=\frac{\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}}{\frac{1}{2 \lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}}$ on solving we get,
$\lambda_{0}=3 \lambda$
39. (c) According to Bohr's theory, the wave number of the last line of the Balmer series in hydrogen spectrum, For hydrogen atom $\mathrm{z}=1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\lambda} & =\mathrm{RZ}^{2}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}^{2}}\right) \\
& =10^{7} \times 1^{2}\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right) \\
\Rightarrow & \text { wave number } \frac{1}{\lambda}=0.25 \times 10^{7} \mathrm{~m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

40. (a) $\alpha$-particles are helium unclei ${ }_{2}^{4} \mathrm{He}$, they are fully ionised and have atomic number 2 are used in Rutherford's scattering experiment also known as Ruther ford's $\alpha$-particle scattering experiment.
41. (d) Let initially there are total $\mathrm{N}_{0}$ number of nuclei

At time $\mathrm{t} \frac{N_{B}}{N_{A}}=0.3$ (given)
$\Rightarrow \quad N_{B}=0.3 N_{A}$
$\mathrm{N}_{0}=N_{A}+N_{B}=N_{A}+0.3 N_{A}$
$\therefore \quad N_{A}=\frac{\mathrm{N}_{0}}{1.3}$
As we know $N_{t}=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$
or, $\frac{\mathrm{N}_{0}}{1.3}=N_{0} e^{-\lambda t}$

$$
\frac{1}{1.3}=e^{-\lambda t} \Rightarrow \ln (1.3)=\lambda t
$$

or, $t=\frac{\ln (1.3)}{\lambda} \Rightarrow t=\frac{\ln (1.3)}{\frac{\ln (2)}{T}}=\frac{\ln (1.3)}{\ln (2)} T$
42. (a) Both answers are correct

Given,
$\lambda_{0}=3250 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$
$\lambda=2536 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m}$
$\phi=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}=\frac{4.14 \times 10^{-15} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{3250 \times 10^{-10}}=3.82 \mathrm{eV}$
$\mathrm{hv}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{4.14 \times 10^{-15} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2536 \times 10^{-10}}=4.89 \mathrm{eV}$
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation,
$\mathrm{K}_{\max }=\mathrm{hv}-\phi$
$\mathrm{KE}_{\text {max }}=(4.89-3.82) \mathrm{eV}=1.077 \mathrm{eV}$
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}=1.077 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{v}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.077 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}}$
or, $\quad \mathrm{v}=0.6 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ or $6 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
43. (b) For last line of Balmer series : $\mathrm{n}_{1}=2$ and $\mathrm{n}_{2}=\infty$
$\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}}=\mathrm{RZ}^{2}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}{ }^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}{ }^{2}}\right]=\mathrm{R}_{1}{ }^{2}\left[\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]$
$\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{4}{\mathrm{R}}$

For last line of Lyman series : $\mathrm{n}_{1}=1$ and $\mathrm{n}_{2}=\infty$
$\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{L}}}=\mathrm{RZ}^{2}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{1}^{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_{2}^{2}}\right]=\mathrm{R} \mathrm{Z}^{2}\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}}-\frac{1}{\infty^{2}}\right]$
$\lambda_{\mathrm{L}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}$

Dividing equation (i) by (ii)
$\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{L}}}=\frac{\frac{4}{\mathrm{R}}}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}}}$
Ratio of wavelengths is $\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{L}}}=4$
44. (a) Given, $\lambda_{\mathrm{A}}=8 \lambda, \lambda_{\mathrm{B}}=\lambda$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& N_{B}=\frac{N_{A}}{e} \Rightarrow N_{o} e^{-\lambda_{B} t}=N_{o} \frac{e^{-\lambda_{A} t}}{e} \\
& e^{-\lambda t}=e^{-8 \lambda t} e^{-1} \\
& e^{-\lambda t}=e^{-8 \lambda t-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparing both side powers

$$
\begin{aligned}
& -\lambda t=-8 \lambda t-1 \\
& -1=7 \lambda t \\
& t=-\frac{1}{7 \lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

The best possible answer is $\mathrm{t}=\frac{1}{7 \lambda}$
45.

$$
\text { (d) } \begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{h} v_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{E}_{\infty}-\mathrm{E}_{1} \\
& \mathrm{~h} v_{\mathrm{f}}=\mathrm{E}_{\infty}-\mathrm{E}_{5} \\
& \mathrm{E} \propto \frac{\mathrm{z}^{2}}{\mathrm{n}^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{E}_{5}}{\mathrm{E}_{1}}=\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{25} \\
& \mathrm{Eqn}(\mathrm{i}) /(\mathrm{ii}) \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{h} v_{\mathrm{L}}}{\mathrm{~h} v_{\mathrm{f}}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{1}}{\mathrm{E}_{5}} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{v_{\mathrm{L}}}{v_{\mathrm{f}}}=\frac{25}{1} \Rightarrow v_{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{v_{\mathrm{L}}}{25}
\end{aligned}
$$

46. (b) In a Bohr orbit of the hydrogen atom

Kinetic energy,

$$
k=\frac{k z e^{2}}{2 r_{n}}
$$

Total energy, $E=\frac{-k z e^{2}}{2 r_{n}}$
So, Kinetic energy : total energy $=1:-1$
47. (c) All nuclei have nearly the same mass density.

Density of nucleus (approx)

$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{M}{V}=\frac{A m_{P}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}}=\frac{A m_{P}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi r_{0}^{3} A} \\
& =\frac{3 m_{P}}{4 \pi r_{0}^{3}}=\text { constant (independent of mass no. A) }
\end{aligned}
$$

48. (b) $\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{3^{2}}\right)=\frac{5 R}{36}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^{2}}-\frac{1}{4^{2}}\right)=\frac{3 R}{16} \therefore \frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}=\frac{80}{108} \\
& \lambda_{2}=\frac{80}{108} \lambda_{1}=\frac{80}{108} \times 660=488.9 \mathrm{~nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

52. (d) de Broglie wavelength

$$
\lambda=\frac{h}{m v} \Rightarrow m=\frac{h}{\lambda v}
$$

Clearly, $m \propto \frac{1}{\lambda v}$
If $\lambda$ and $v$ be the wavelength and velocity of electron and $\lambda^{\prime}$ and $v^{\prime}$ be the wavelength and velocity of the particle then

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{m^{\prime}}{m}=\frac{v \lambda}{v^{\prime} \lambda^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{5} \times \frac{1}{1.878} \times 10^{-4} \\
& \Rightarrow m=9.7 \times 10^{-28} \mathrm{~kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

53. (c) For photoelectric emission, photoelectric current, incident light frequency should be greater than threshold frequency.
Light of frequency 1.5 times the threshold frequency $v_{0}$ incident.

$$
v=\frac{3}{2} v_{0}
$$

If frequency is halved,

$$
\therefore v^{\prime}=\frac{v}{2}=\frac{3}{4} v_{0} \quad \because v^{\prime}<v_{0}
$$

85. ( 449 years) Let at time $t$, numbers of atoms of the radioctive substance are N .
It may decay either by $\alpha$-emission or by $\beta$-emission.

$$
\left(-\frac{d N}{d t}\right)_{n e t}=\left(-\frac{d N}{d t}\right)_{\alpha}+\left(-\frac{d N}{d t}\right)_{\beta}
$$

If, decay constant is $\lambda$, then

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \quad \lambda \mathrm{N}=\lambda_{\alpha} \mathrm{N}+\lambda_{\beta} \mathrm{N} \\
& \lambda=\lambda_{\alpha}+\lambda_{\beta}=\frac{1}{1620}+\frac{1}{405}=\frac{1}{324} \text { year }^{-1} \\
& \text { As } \mathrm{N}=\mathrm{N}_{0} \mathrm{e}^{-\lambda \mathrm{t}} \\
& \text { Here, } N=\frac{N_{0}}{4} \\
& \therefore-\lambda \mathrm{t}=\ell \mathrm{n}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)=-1.386 \quad\left(\frac{1}{324}\right) t=1.386 \\
& \qquad \mathrm{t}=449 \text { years }
\end{aligned}
$$

86. (7.73) Mass defect $=\left[3 \times m_{p}+4 \times m_{n}\right]-7 u$

Binding energy $=$ mass defect $\times 931.5 \mathrm{MeV}$
Binding energy per nucleon $=\frac{\text { Binding energy }}{7}$
87. $(8,6)$ The equation of decay process is

$$
{ }_{92} U^{238} \rightarrow{ }_{82} P b^{206}+n\left({ }_{2} H e^{4}\right)+m\left({ }_{-1} \beta^{\circ}\right)
$$

Here n and m are the numbers of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ particles respectively. By conservation of mass

$$
206+n(4) \quad=\quad 238
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad n & =8 \\
\text { Also } 82+2 n+m(-1) & =92 \\
\text { or } 82+2 \times 8-m & =92 \\
\therefore \quad m & =6
\end{aligned}
$$

So, $x=8, y=6$
88. $(172,69)$ The process can be expressed as :

$$
{ }_{72} \mathrm{~A}^{180} \xrightarrow{\alpha}{ }_{70} \mathrm{~A}_{1}^{176} \xrightarrow{\beta^{-}}{ }_{71} \mathrm{~A}_{2}^{176}
$$

$$
\xrightarrow{\alpha}{ }_{69} \mathrm{~A}_{3}^{172} \xrightarrow{\gamma}{ }_{69} \mathrm{~A}_{4}^{172}
$$

Thus, the mass number of $\mathrm{A}_{4}$ is 172 .
89. (56.45) If $\mathrm{N}_{0}$ is the initial level of radiation and N is the permissible level, then

$$
\begin{aligned}
N & =\frac{N_{0}}{50} . \\
\text { We know that } \quad N & =N_{0} e^{-\lambda t} \\
\text { where } \quad \lambda & =\frac{0.693}{t_{1 / 2}} \\
& =\frac{0.693}{10}=0.0693 \mathrm{day}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

From equation (1), we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{N_{0}}{50} & =N_{0} e^{-0.0693 t} \\
t & =56.45 \text { days }
\end{aligned}
$$

or
90. $\left(1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right) E=W_{0}+K_{\text {max }}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& E=\frac{h v}{\lambda}=\frac{12375}{3000}=4.12 \mathrm{eV} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad K_{\max }=E-W_{0}=4.12 \mathrm{eV}-1 \mathrm{eV}=3.125 \mathrm{eV} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} m_{e} V_{\max }^{2}=3.12 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad V_{\max } \approx 1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}
$$
